A main point is to gather statistics on households (defined as people who live and eat together), as well as getting an overview of the distribution of persons and households by size of income, socio-economic groups, household type, geography etc. Another objective is to collect income and fortune data as a backdrop for the Level of Living and Consumer surveys. Information is gathered on all types of income, fortune, taxes, income for individuals and households. Some information is added from other registers (amongst others, marital status and family composition), whilst information on household composition is gathered through interviews. In addition, one generally includes information on education, profession and occupation for individuals, and composition, type and primary income recipients for the households. 

There exist both a household file and a person file. These are documented separately. This is the person file for 2004.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 2003
description:
Statistics Norway has used the periodic Consumer Expenditure Surveys as the basis to develop and update the weight basis for the Consumer Price Index. Detailed overview of the consumption in private households has been an important foundation for the living conditions statistics, og for research on income, living conditions and for example the effects of the taxation systems use of direct and indirect taxes. The material is also detailed enough to provide an interesting basis for dietary analysis. The data kan even be used for demand studies, in financial planning models or for the specification of the private consumption in the national accounts.

In 1974, Statistics Norway started doing yearly surveys with a somewhat smaller sample. From 1974-1976, the data is publish for three years at the time, and the yearly gross sample is about 1500 households with a net sample of 1100-1200 households. The goal was to continue to build a detailed overview of the consumption in private households, as the basis for updating the weight basis for the Consumer Price Index. In addition, one wishes to map the consumption of different types of households. The households are classified by variables such as household type, income, the employment status of the main income provider in the household, age, residential areas, size of total consumption expenditures, etc. Data are organized in several parts og contains background information about the household (collected through a introductory interview which maps the household composition), detailed consumption measured in value and amount (registered in diaries for the household as a whole, as well as the individuals it consists of), the households purchases of lasting consumer goods (lasting capital goods, collected in an extensive exit interview), consumption of self-made goods, income and tax.

title: KEU Follow-up Survey 2003
description:
The present follow-up study is among those who participated in the preliminary survey in 2004 (2003 cohort) and is based on interviews with 759 companies distributed across 21 different services. The 21 services are divided into three main groups, where group 1 is financial services, group 2 offers both financing, advisory, and networking services, while group 3 is entrepreneur and incubator grants.

For the first time, programs are included in the customer impact survey. This means that commitments made through services often associated with competence development, networking, and innovation are investigated simultaneously with purely financial measures still included in the survey.

Innovation Norway was established on January 1, 2004. The companies that received services in 2003 had a customer relationship with either SND, the Norwegian Export Council, the Norwegian Tourist Board, or the State Guidance Office for Inventors. For simplicity, Innovation Norway (IN) is used as a collective term for these in the questionnaire for the 2003 survey.

In this survey, it is mainly services from SND that have been evaluated. The only exception to this is the INT program from the former Norwegian Export Council.

title: Survey of Living Conditions and Psychological Health Among Inmates in Norwegian Prisons, 2003
description: In June 2003, Statistics Norway (SSB), at division for interviews, carried out a national interview survey concerning the living conditions and psychological health among inmates in Norwegian prisons. SSB conducted the survey on behalf of the Research Foundation Fafo. The survey was financed by the Ministry of Justice and the Police and the Norwegian Directorate of Health. The purpose of the survey was to gather new knowledge and insight into the living conditions of inmates.

title: Local Elections in Norway, 1999 - 2003, panel
description:
The data set is derived from Statistic Norway's local election survey municipal and county council elections in 1999 and 2003, commissioned by Institute of Social Research. The purpose of the survey was to enlighten different sides of the local democracy; elections and election campaigns, political interest and participation. 

The survey deals with the following subjects:
-Political interest
-Voting in municipal, county council and national elections.
-Assessment of the significance of elections
-Assessment of the local democracy and local services
-Municipal challenges
-Municipal organisation
-Information about politics
-Voting for persons in municipal elections
-Political participation

The survey is conducted as telephone interview and self-completion forms. The variables from the telephone interview have names beginning with 'spm', whilst variables from the self-completion questionnaire have variable names starting with 'po'.

To obtain knowledge about changes in the electorate, a part of the sample is derived from the gross sample of the Local Election Surveys in 1995 and 1999 respectively. These panels consists of people in the ages of 22 - 83 years. For the panel to have cross-sectional features, the real panel had to be supplied with persons from cohorts that recently had become eligible to vote during the four intermittent years. The supplementary sample is often characterized as a "false" panel. Variables that started with 'l' are derived from the Local Election Survey of 1999.

title: Local Elections in Norway 2003
description: The data set is derived from Statistic Norway's election survey for municipal and county council elections in 2003, commissioned by Institute of Social Research. The purpose of the survey was to enlighten different sides of the locaø democracy; elections and election campaigns, political interest and participation. The survey deals with the following subjects:-Political interest-Voting in municipal, county council and national elections.-Assessment of the significance of elections-Assessment of the local democracy and local services-Municipal challenges-Municipal organisation-Information about politics-Voting for persons in municipal elections-Political participationThe survey is conducted as telephone interview and self-completion forms. The variables from the telephone interview have names beginning with 'spm', whilst variables from the self-completion questionnaire have variable names starting with 'po'.To obtain knowledge about changes in the electorate, a part of the sample is derived from the gross sample of the Local Election Survey in 1999. This panel consists of people in the ages of 22 - 83 years. For the panel to have cross-sectional features, the real panel had to be supplied with persons from cohorts that recently had become eligible to vote during the four intermittent years. The supplementary sample is often characterized as a "false" panel. Variables that started with 'l' are derived from the Local Election Survey of 1999.

title: Survey on future plans, familiy and marriage 2003
description: Statistics Norway's (SSB) section for interview surveys conducted a post-enquete about future plans, family and cohabitation, in the period January to May, 2003. SSB's section for demography and living conditions funded the survey. Turid Noack and Torkild Lyngstad were contact persons. The aim of the survey was to gather new knowledge about how various welfare initiatives can be adjusted for changing family structures. The questions are concerned with how singles and couples organize their daily lifes. Attitudes towards various family issues are also adressed.

title: Labour Force Survey 1. Quarter 2002 - 4. Quarter 2003, Panel
description:
Labour Force Survey 1. Quarter 2002 - 4. Quarter 2003, Panel (8 quarters)

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). title: Labour Force Survey 2003, Year file
description:
Labour Force Survey 2003 - merged year file.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level. 

------------------------------------------------------
Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development. 

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics. 

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned. 

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987. 
------------------------------------------------------------- 
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Labour Force Survey 2003, 4th quarter
description:
Labour Force Survey 2003 - 4th quarter.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level. 

------------------------------------------------------
Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development. 

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics. 

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned. 

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987. 
------------------------------------------------------------- 
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Children's Level of Living in Low-income Families, 2003
description:
The study of children's level of living in low-income families were conducted on behalf of NOVA (Norwegian Social Research). The survey is part of a research project on children and young people's living conditions and welfare, that NOVA and the Norwegian Women's Public Health Association (NKS) announced an agreement on in 2000.

The aim of this study on children in low-income families is to obtain knowledge about the relationship between parents' finances and children's living conditions and daily life in their leisure, at school and at home. The focus is on children's experiences related to growing up in low-income families.

The survey is scheduled for three rounds - 2003, 2006 and 2009. The panel in 2006 and 2009 will consist of those who responded in 2003 that has not migrated or died. In 2003, the age group 6-9 years were interviewed indirectly through parents.

title: Guidance and skills development in the labour market, 2001
description:
In today's society workers meet demands to competence which largely involves the ability to adapt in response to new tasks and challenges. They have to grow and learn in their daily work to gain access to employment - where the demands of the workers are constantly changing. This project focuses on the learning and skills needed in the workplace. It focuses on the educational and training needs of employees in a range of companies within automotive, health, crafts and trade businesses. Two groups are given special attention: immigrants and apprentices. The project deals with the results of a survey, interviews and observational studies.

Various sectors have been studied with respect to employees' background, affiliation to the work and knowledge of potential trainers and training programs in the workplace.

It was found that for the workers space to act and relationships were important. One found that characteristics such as belonging, freedom, working and influence were characteristics that were considered more important than characteristics such as career, creativity and commitment.

title: Income Study 2004, person file
description:
Statistics Norway (SSB) conducted wide-ranging Income Studies in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. As of 1984, SSB moved to a system of annual surveys. This entailed changes, more in terms of sample size and sampling method than the type of information that was gathered. The information is mainly decided by what is available in public tax assessment records.

The Income Studies should not be considered ordinary sample surveys, they are based on a sample drawn from registers from the Norwegian equivalent to the Internal Revenue Service. This means that foreign citizens would be part of the sample if they are registered in the Central Person Register, which they will normally be if they have valid work and residence permits. Children born during the year in question will be included in the sample regardless of birth date, and so will persons dying that the year.

The objective of the Income Studies have been to elucidate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A main point is to gather statistics on households that live and eat together, as well as getting an overview of the distribution of persons and households by size of income, socio-economic groups, household type, geography etc. Another objective is to collect income and fortune data as a backdrop for the Level of Living and Consumer surveys. Information is gathered om all types of income, fortune, taxes, income for individuals and households. Some information is added from other registers (amongst others, marital status and family composition, whilst information on household composition is gathered through interviews. In addition, one generally includes information on education, profession and occupation for individuals and household composition, type and primary income recipients for the households. 

There exist both a household file and a person file. These are documented separately. This is the person file.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1986-2003, Household Level Data - Income
description:
Statistics Norway have conducted comprehensive Income and wealth studies in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984 and onwards, Statistics Norway changed to a system of yearly surveys, the change was mainly the size of the sample and problems concerning sample selection, and not so much about the information collected. The information collected is mainly decided by the information available in public tax assessment data. The income and wealth studies should not be considered normal sample surveys, as they are based on a sample drawn from the Norwegian Tax Administrations registers. That means that foreign nationals are included if they are registered in the central personal data register, which they normally would be if they have working and residence permits. Children born in the course of the year will be included no matter the date of birth and likewise will persons that died in the course of the year. 

The aim of the Income and Wealth surveys has been to shed light on the income conditions for the whole population and for different groups. One main objective is to produce statistics of households that live and eat together and to provide an overview of the distribution of persons and household by income size, socioeconomic groupings, household types, geography and so forth. Another main objective is to collect income and wealth data as a background for the survey on living conditions and consumer expenditure surveys. In addition, in the years following 1992, a reform of the taxation system had been conducted, which also made it important to collect information in order to study the implications of the reform, also for self-employed persons, and an integration of a larger study of these conditions were included especially. Information concerning all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, disposable income for single persons and for households were collected. Some information is connected from other registers, which includes among other things marital status and family composition, while information concerning household composition was obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower term than household is, a family can consist of singles, an unmarried father or mother with children, or married couples with or without children. A household consists of, on the other hand, all persons that live and eat together, and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows that approximately 90 % of all households only consists of one family. (Notater 98/11 SSB 1998: Inntekts- og formuesundersøkelsen 1995). Tax-exempted income, as for example benefits of a social character, gifts and winnings are not included because of the tax assessment regulations. Rules for imputed rental value of property, valuation of benefits in kind, private withdrawals, devaluations and so forth are also creating some problems. 

This cumulative file contains data from the income and wealth studies for the income years 1986-2003. The units are households. Further, the dataset is divided into one file for income and one file for wealth. This file contains data concerning income.

title: Community and public health medicine in Norway - Telephone interviews 2001-2002
description:
Telephone Interviews with the mayor, city manager, public health practicioner and general practitioner in respectively 90 and 60 municipalities (2001 and 2002), a total of 360 and 240 people.

The objective of this study was to describe and explore the local public health's work and framework over time, in relation to workload, work content and mobility in the labor market. Furthermore, how were primary physicians' tasks prioritized before and after implementation of the GP scheme, with special emphasis on the compound preventive health care in the municipalities.

title: From consensus to conflict and variety? On the increase in user-directed personal assistance - In depth interviews, 2002
description: User-controlled personal assistance (BPA) has since 1994 had trial status through state action plans for the disabled. The number of users has increased rapidly until today. 1 May 2000 a change in the Social Services Act came in effect, stating that BPA is an initiative local authorities are required to have among its services. Tensions and conflicts of interest related to BPA which has scarcely been expressed in the trial, is expected to get stronger upon scaling up the service. Tensions have only rarely been taken up in connection with legislating BPA and will be reflected in the implementation by the solutions of which are left to the municipalities. Tensions can in various ways be linked to issues of whether BPA should be a system where the user control is kept strictly upheld or whether to develop pragmatic solutions that can be customized to wider audiences and municipal organization and practice. Specifically, they will be expressed in relation to questions about the scope and composition of the BPA, how the target group is delimited, how BPA is organized and how the assistants are recruited and what kind of working conditions they are provided. The project will elucidate through surveys and interviews with users of BPA and municipalities implementing the scheme.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2002
description:
Statistics Norway have used periodical consumption surveys as a basis for establishing and updating the weighting foundation for the reality price index. Detailed accounts of the consumption in private households have also established an important basis for the level of living statistics and for research on income, level of living and for example effects of the taxing system usage of direct and indirect taxes. The material is also so detailed that it constitutes an interesting groundwork for nutrition analyses. The data can also be applied in demand studies, in economical planning models or for specification of private consumption in the national financial statement. 


In 1974 SSB converted to a system with annual surveys with somewhat smaller samples. From 1974-1976 data was published for 3 years at the time, and the annual gross samples of about 1500 households, net sample of 1100-1200 households. The aim was still to build a detailed overview of the consumption in private households, that constitutes a groundwork for updating the weighting basis in the reality price index. Another aim was to map out consumption in various types of households. The households are classified in accordance with such marks as household type, income, main income earners working status and age, neighborhood, size of total consumption spending, etc. The data are organized in several sections and contains background information about the household (gathered through an introductory interview that maps out the household composition), detailed consumption measured in value and amounts (registered by journal notation, for the household and the individual members of the household), the households purchase of durable consumer goods (lasting capital objects, gathered through a comprehensive finishing interview), consumption of self-produced goods, income and taxes.

title: Labour Force Surveys, 2. Quarter 2000 - 1. Quarter 2002, Panel
description:
Labour Force Surveys, 2. Quarter 2000 - 1. Quarter 2002, Panel

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new Norwegian standard classification of occupations (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

Every second quarter AKU is extended with a number of questions to collect data accordant to Eurostats / EUs spesifications.

From 1996, the rotation schedule changed, each family now attends eight consecutive quarters. As of 1996 the selection shedule was changed as well with stratification at the county level.

Summary of AKU panels with detailed explanations for panel files:
Data collection for the new rotation plan as the basis for this file is shown below. Participants in the 4th quarter of 1997 is the first batch of 8 possible completed interviews.


Pool        |1996|1997|1998|1999|2000|2001|2002|2003|
Quarter  |1234|1234|1234|1234|1234|1234|1234|1234|

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------


Panel files are created by linking eight interviews from an ordinary interview round of the Labour Force Survey. In the table above means that one could connect all the batches (lines) marked X.
Participation 8 times used as a criterion for selection.

Annual files and quarterly files consist of respondents who are participating for 1st time, 2nd time, and so on. The weights allow such cross-sections (columns) can be balanced with national figures. If 4 quarters is merged into an annual volume, the quarterly weights be divided by four to attain correct weighted annual numbers.

Each panel (panel files: horisontal in the table) will have a weight compiled for each quarter. Although the weights do not differ much from each other, they will be compiled independently, and each weight should be used only for the data which the quarterly weight is made for. Weighted numbers is valid for one pool, which means ca 12,5 % (1/8) of a total quarterly selection. Therefore, one must multiply by 8 to approximate national quarterly figures

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation.

title: Labour Force Survey 2002, 2nd quarter
description:
Labour Force Survey 2002 - 2nd quarter.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level. 

------------------------------------------------------
Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development. 

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics. 

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned. 

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987. 
------------------------------------------------------------- 
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Norwegian Election Survey 2001
description: The Norwegian Election Research Programme was launched in the middle of the 1950's, under the direction of The Institute for Social Research. Henry Valen and Stein Rokkan were responsible for the programme. The programme cooperated with Norsk Gallup Institute A/S for sample survey data collection in the election studies covering the Storting elections in 1957, 1965, 1969 and 1973. Since 1977 the programme has cooperated with Statistics Norway (SSB) for the data collection for these studies. The election studies are intended to give statistical data for research covering elections and political processes in Norway. Since the start of the Norwegian Election Programme Professor Henry Valen has been the key administrator, but in 1985 Bernt Aardal took over as executive manager.The 2001 survey was conducted in two parts, one survey before the election and another survey after the elcection, where the same sample was interviewed at both times. The background for this is that one wants to discover more about how the election campaign, and especially the media coverage of it, will affect the voters' preferences and behaviour. This is a merged file consisting of both the survey before and after the elecetion, with all valid units included.

title: From consensus to conflict and variety? On the increase in user-directed personal assistance - Survey, 2002
description: User-controlled personal assistance (BPA) has since 1994 had trial status through state action plans for the disabled. The number of users has increased rapidly until today. 1 May 2000 a change in the Social Services Act came in effect, stating that BPA is an initiative local authorities are required to have among its services. Tensions and conflicts of interest related to BPA which has scarcely been expressed in the trial, is expected to get stronger upon scaling up the service. Tensions have only rarely been taken up in connection with legislating BPA and will be reflected in the implementation by the solutions of which are left to the municipalities. Tensions can in various ways be linked to issues of whether BPA should be a system where the user control is kept strictly upheld or whether to develop pragmatic solutions that can be customized to wider audiences and municipal organization and practice. Specifically, they will be expressed in relation to questions about the scope and composition of the BPA, how the target group is delimited, how BPA is organized and how the assistants are recruited and what kind of working conditions they are provided. The project will elucidate through surveys and interviews with users of BPA and municipalities implementing the scheme.

title: Income Study 2002, household file
description:
Statistics Norway (SSB) conducted wide-ranging Income Studies in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. As of 1984, SSB moved to a system of annual surveys. This entailed changes, more in terms of sample size and sampling method than the type of information that was gathered. The information is mainly decided by what is available in public tax assessment records.

The Income Studies should not be considered ordinary sample surveys, they are based on a sample drawn from registers from the Norwegian equivalent to the Internal Revenue Service. This means that foreign citizens would be part of the sample if they are registered in the Central Person Register, which they will normally be if they have valid work and residence permits. Children born during the year in question will be included in the sample regardless of birth date, and so will persons dying that the year.

The objective of the Income Studies have been to elucidate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A main point is to gather statistics on households that live and eat together, as well as getting an overview of the distribution of persons and households by size of income, socio-economic groups, household type, geography etc. Another objective is to collect income and fortune data as a backdrop for the Level of Living and Consumer surveys. Information is gathered om all types of income, fortune, taxes, income for individuals and households. Some information is added from other registers (amongst others, marital status and family composition, whilst information on household composition is gathered through interviews. In addition, one generally includes information on education, profession and occupation for individuals and household composition, type and primary income recipients for the households. 

There exist both a household file and a person file. These are documented separately. This is the household file.

title: Improving public health control of sexually transmitted diseases in developing countries, 2001
description: In a cross-sectional study, 703 antenatal care (ANC) attendees were interviewed and examined, and specimens were collected to identify the prevalence of Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Trichomonas vaginalis, bacterial vaginosis, Candida species and syphilis.

title: Time use survey 2000/01, interview file
description:
The objective of "Time Budget Survey 2000/01" is to gather infomation on how the population spends its time doing different activities. The time busget survey is our primary source of knowledge about the amount and forms of unpaid work that are carried out in society, who does this work and when it is done.  The survey contains data on how people actually live within the restraints and oppurtunities provided by their levels of living. Thus, it constitutes a valuable supplement to the level of living surveys. 

The Time Budget Survey was firstly conducted in Norway in 1972-73. It was originally inspired by the international survey "Comparative Time-Budget Projecet", conducted in twelce different countries. The survey from 2000-01 is the fourth of its kind in Norway and was designed to ensure comparability with the international survey.

The data material is mainly collected through written diaries. Also, participants were asked to answer questions in a personal interview. 

The survey deals with questions on use of time within the following domains:
1. Work
2. Work-related travels
3. Domestic work, maintenance, child care, shopping and personal travels
4. Personal needs
5. Education
6. Leisure activities (sports, entertainment, social activities, media and reading)

This dataset contains the data for the interviews. The diaries are documented in a separate file.

title: Labour Force Survey, 1. Quarter 2000 - 4. Quarter 2001, Panel
description:
Labour Force Survey, 1. Quarter 2000 - 4. Quarter 2001, Panel

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new Norwegian standard classification of occupations (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

Every second quarter AKU is extended with a number of questions to collect data accordant to Eurostats / EUs spesifications.


From 1996, the rotation schedule changed, each family now attends eight consecutive quarters. As of 1996 the selection shedule was changed as well with stratification at the county level.

Panel files are created by linking eight interviews from an ordinary interview round of the Labour Force Survey. In the table above means that one could connect all the batches (lines) marked X.
Participation 8 times used as a criterion for selection.

Annual files and quarterly files consist of respondents who are participating for 1st time, 2nd time, and so on. The weights allow such cross-sections (columns) can be balanced with national figures. If 4 quarters is merged into an annual volume, the quarterly weights be divided by four to attain correct weighted annual numbers.

Each panel (panel files: horisontal in the table) will have a weight compiled for each quarter. Although the weights do not differ much from each other, they will be compiled independently, and each weight should be used only for the data which the quarterly weight is made for. Weighted numbers is valid for one pool, which means ca 12,5 % (1/8) of a total quarterly selection. Therefore, one must multiply by 8 to approximate national quarterly figures

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation.

title: Labour Force Survey 2001, 3rd quarter
description:
Labour Force Survey 2001 - 3rd quarter.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level. 

------------------------------------------------------
Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development. 

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics. 

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned. 

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987. 
------------------------------------------------------------- 
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Labour Force Survey 2001, 4th quarter
description:
Labour Force Survey 2001 - 4th quarter.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level. 

------------------------------------------------------
Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development. 

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics. 

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned. 

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987. 
------------------------------------------------------------- 
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: NSD1319
description:
The dataset is derived from the Norwegian part of the international project OASIS (Old age and autonomy - the role of services systems and intergenerational family solidarity). It was conducted by NOVA, financed by EU and the Research Council of Norway. The project was a collaboration between five countries and many scientists. NOVA's partners were Haifa University and ESHEL, Israel; Deutsches Zentrum für Altersfragen, Berlin, Tyskland; Universidad del País Vasco, Bilbao, Spania; and University of Keele, England. Prohject coordinator was professor Ariela Lowenstein, Haifa University.

The main theme of the survey was the family's role in the welfare state. The five countries that were included in the survey all have different family cultures and welfare politics. A random sample of the population in the big cities from 25 years and older were interviewed, about 1200 in each country. In Norway, the sample was drawn from Oslo, Bergen and Trondheim.

title: Leadership Survey 2000
description:
In 1997, the Norwegian parliament decided to conduct a national democratic audit in Norway. The "Leadership survey", from which this dataset is derived from, constitutes a central part of this audit. The survey is conducted by Statistics Norway (SSB), and the Institute of Social Research (ISF) helped to coordinate.
The purpose of the Leadership survey has been to get insight in background and carreer pattern, contacts, the relationship between leaders in different societal areas, as well as the attitudes towards central social issues. 

The survey is divided into 6 parts:
1. Background and carreer
2. Perceptions and attituted
3. Gender equality
4. Contacts (generally and case-oriented)
5. Attitudes towards the political system and international cooperation
6. Media

The datamaterial is based on interviews of leaders within the following ten sectors:
1.  Research and higher education
2.  Defense
3.  Central administration
4.  Church
5.  Culture
6.  Media
7.  Business
8.  Organisations
9.  Politics
10. Police and judicial organs

title: Elderly care in big cities. Evaluation of the action plan for the elderly 1998-2001 - care needing elderly people's experiences
description:
The survey was conducted in order to evaluate "the Action plan for welfare services for the elderly 1998-2001". 

1000 elderly people in Oslo, Kristiansand, Bergen, Trondheim and Tromsø were contacted and asked how they were doing. 570 persons gave consent to be interviewed. The aim was to study experiences care givers and care receivers have with regard to how nursing and care services functions. Please note that 3 different questionnaires were used, one for those living in nursing homes, one for those who lived in care homes and one for those elderly people living at home. The answers of all three groups are incorporated in this file.
The aim was to study experiences the employed have with how nursing and care services in Oslo, Bergen, Trondheim, Kristiansand og Tromsø work.

When the Action plan for welfare services for the elderly 1998-2001 was adopted (19th of June 1997) the National Assembly of Norway (Stortinget) demanded an evaluation. Several research projects evaluating different parts of the action plan were initiated. These projects are finances by the Research Council of Norway.

title: The welfare state and local authorities. Evaluation of the action plan for the elderly 1998-2001
description:
The project is a study of the implementation of "the Action plan for welfare services for the elderly" in the 24 municipalities of the county Nord-Trøndlag. Both the processes the action plan put in motion in the municipalities, and the specific results of it are investigated. Focus has been on the conditions the municipalities had to carry out the Action plan, internal discussions in the municipalities with regard to different solutions and the relationship between different municipal initiatives and governmental steering. Here the speed of development in the new initiatives were emphasized, whihch buildings that were chosen and where these were built.

The Action plan for welfare services for the elderly's goals were to strengthen the home based services, develop further the care home sector, increase the nursing home capacity and arrange for single rooms in nursing homes for all that wanted it. All these golas were reached on a national level, something which is rare in such big national reforms. However it turned out that the central authorities' ideas about functionality and running of new care homes and nursing homes were modified. In the report "Velferdsstat og velferdskommune. Iverksettingen av «Handlingsplan for eldreomsorgen» i kommunene i Nord-Trøndelag" it is documented how theses ideals changed when facing everyday reality in the municipalities of Nord-Trøndelag county.


The dataset is made up of four components:
                   1- Field studies
                   2- Interviews with local decision-makers in the municipality
                   3- Information on service receivers in institutions and homes.
                   4- Data on the municipalities from public registers.

title: How Accurate and Complete are the Contents of General Practitioners' Electronic Medical Record System? 2000
description:
The background for "How Accurate and Complete are the Contents of General Practitioners' Electronic Medical Record System?, 2000" was the improvement potential the data material in electronic pasient journals (EPJs) had, in addition to research on epidemiology and further societal medicine. It was necessary to know more about the extents of the EPJ data, and how this data material could be compiled with what all involved agents knew about the patient. Therefore the project had several objectives: 1) To evaluate the completeness of the data material in EPJs. Was any data missing? 2) Evaluate the correctness of the data material in EPJs. Was the data correct? 3) Describe variations concerning eight frequent diagnosis, between medical offices. 4) Evaluate the practitioner's satisfaction with our feedback and software. 

We could by the use of a program called QTools extract fields from EPJs. To find out how extensive the EPJ was, we counted blank fields. To retrieve information concerning correctness, we used several methods. For example we tied together different EPJ fields, for example diagnosis and medication, to identify each patient with a particular medical condition. Short informal interviews with practitioners and employees gave us more information concerning the data of the practices. The data material in practitioners' EPJs were very extensive, except for some fields (for example smoking, habits and field of work), but to interpret the correctness, a great caution was needed. A large part of the EPJs could be used for quality improvement and research, but one would then have to analyze the data material in close cooperation with the practices.

title: Income Study 2001, household file
description:
Statistics Norway (SSB) conducted wide-ranging Income Studies in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. As of 1984, SSB moved to a system of annual surveys. This entailed changes, more in terms of sample size and sampling method than the type of information that was gathered. The information is mainly decided by what is available in public tax assessment records.

The Income Studies should not be considered ordinary sample surveys, they are based on a sample drawn from registers from the Norwegian equivalent to the Internal Revenue Service. This means that foreign citizens would be part of the sample if they are registered in the Central Person Register, which they will normally be if they have valid work and residence permits. Children born during the year in question will be included in the sample regardless of birth date, and so will persons dying that the year.

The objective of the Income Studies have been to elucidate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A main point is to gather statistics on households that live and eat together, as well as getting an overview of the distribution of persons and households by size of income, socio-economic groups, household type, geography etc. Another objective is to collect income and fortune data as a backdrop for the Level of Living and Consumer surveys. Information is gathered om all types of income, fortune, taxes, income for individuals and households. Some information is added from other registers (amongst others, marital status and family composition, whilst information on household composition is gathered through interviews. In addition, one generally includes information on education, profession and occupation for individuals and household composition, type and primary income recipients for the households. 

There exist both a household file and a person file. These are documented separately. This is the household file.

title: Time use survey, 2000/01, diary file
description:
The Time use survey is a survey of people's activities and interaction patterns. A representative sample of the population has led diaries and recorded what they do and who they are with. They are also interviewed in relation to the keeping of the diary. It is recorded in diaries for 12 months. The time use Survey 2000-01, Statistics Norway has conducted four such surveys. The three last conducted in 1971-72, 1980-81 and 1990-91.
The time use survey is our most important source for knowledge about how much and what type of unpaid work is performed in the population, who performs this work and when it is performed. Also the survey is the only souce to data about the population's daily rythm,about spare time activities and about time spent with offspring and other family. Paid work is also covered in other surveys, but in the time use survey this can be studied in relation to time use in general.
The time use survey complements eg. the survey of living conditions in that it reveals how people actually live within the possibilities and limitations livelihoods provides. The living conditions survey and the time use survey first and foremost maps main conditions and relations. They say less about how people experience everyday, what their desires and motives are or what certain activities mean to the individual. Investigations of action frames, actual pattern of behavior and the importance of the action of the individual are three different but complementary ways of describing society.
Time use surveys provide a picture of people's daily lives. Results from several points in time therefore tells how Norwegians way of living changes. Comparisons across national borders shows how Norwegians differ from people in other countries.

title: Can’t Somebody Read for Me? Evaluation of Three Subprojects Affiliated with the Signal Project “Troll i ord”, 2000
description: The purpose of this survey was to evaluate three reading stimulation projects aimed at children from the age 2 to 6. The projects were three of “Troll i ord”s subprojects that intended to stimulate love of reading among children that had not begun formal reading education.  The target groups were 2-year-olds, children in kindergarten and first graders and the reading stimulation should take place primarily by the help of the child's parents and by reading aloud.  The evaluation survey is directed primarily towards the parents (questionnaire). Because “Troll i ord” also is a signal project, the evaluation survey also included other informants that had been involved in carrying out the subprojects (interview). Data from the evaluation survey showed generally a positive effect of the reading stimulation.

title: Level of Living 2000 - Panel Survey
description: This data set is taken from the Level of Living Survey, that was first conducted in 1997. The panel surveys was conducted in 1997, 1998, 1999 and 2000. Questionnaires and the execution of the survey was prepared by Statistics Norway. The theme of studies is mainly health, housing conditions and working conditions, subjects that correspond to the annual cross-sectional surveys. The gross sample for the survey was approximately 5000 people aged 16-80 years. Since it is a panel survey, it was sought to maintain as many as possible of the same respondents. However, it was supplemented with new respondents on the basis of apostasy and representativeness of the youngest age group (16-year-olds). The original panel through all the survey was 3059 persons. The net response rate was between 79-75%. The panel survey has the advantage that it can follow the progress of the people interviewed in the previous studies, although it was maintained a distinction between follow-up interview and the initial interview.

title: Labour Force Survey, 2. quarter 1998 - 1. quarter 2000, panel
description:
As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research.

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new Norwegian standard classification of occupations (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.   

Every second quarter AKU is extended with a number of questions to collect data accordant to Eurostats / EUs spesifications.

From 1996, the rotation schedule changed, each family now attends eight consecutive quarters. As of 1996 the selection shedule was changed as well with stratification at the county level. 

Panel files are created by linking eight interviews from an ordinary interview round of the Labour Force Survey.

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation.

title: Labour Force Survey, 1. quarter 1999 - 4. quarter 2000, panel
description:
This dataset is based on the panel survey "Labour Force Survey, 1. quarter 1999 - 4. quarter 2000, panel".
The Labour Force Survey will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. The Labour Force Survey has evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1996, the LFS questionnaire, file description, and standard coding of industry and occupation was significaly revised. Data collection eventually went over to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. The present file has incorporated these changes. New industrial classification in this file. (NOS C 182, based on EU standard NACE, Rev.1) New Classification of Occupations (STRYK) based on ISCO 88 is adopted from 1996, in these data the occupation are coded for new standard.

From 1996 the rotation schedule changed, so every family now is attending eight consecutive quarters. From 1996, also the sampling plan changed, making stratification at the county level.
