title: Cooperation Between After School Programs and Voluntary Organizations and Teams, 1995
description:
This project aims to map out and evaluate the cooperation between after school programs and voluntary organizations. The project was initiated by Fellessekretariatet for after school programs in Ministry of Church Affairs, Education and Research. 
A questionnaire was sent to a selection of school and cultural government departments in the municipalities, as well as to primary schools that had established an after school program. Information was gathered about the distribution of goals and content in the cooperation between the voluntary organizations and the after school programs, and assessments of this cooperation.

NSD has prepared data from the survey part of the project. Data from interviews with employees in after school programs, students, instructors, and other actors in voluntary organizations on a local level can not be made available.

title: Children in Residential Care - a Segregated Group in Education, 1994-95
description:
The purpose of the project was to map elementary education of children and young people living in county child welfare institutions. The reason is that the county, not the primary municipality is responsible for primary schooling for children living in institutions. The project was initiated by the child welfare institutions' cooperation forum. The aim was to identify where students got their education, organization of teaching, expert knowledge, individual decisions, individual learning plans, cooperation between school / institution, quality of services and school and institution employees' ratings of the offer. Questionnaires were answered by staff of the institution, elementary school teacher, executive officer of the municipality and officer in the counties education agencies. Some interviews have also been conducted, but they are not suitable for use by others.

The study contains four data sets.

title: Religious Identity and Transmission of Religious Traditions in a Minority Situation, 1995.
description:
The aim of this project is: 
1) identifying key opinion and affiliation aspect of religiosity and religious world view among Vietnamese-Norwegian children, youngsters and adults belonging to the Catholic and Buddhist community within the Vietnamese minority community in a part of Rogaland.
- What these various religious groups believe, and how their faith is expressed in social life
- How the faith gets its culturally specific expression in the meeting between Norwegian and Vietnamese culture and within the framework of the structural condition given by the Norwegian and the West Norwegian exile lifestyle
- How Vietnamese-Norwegian Catholicism and Buddhism is communicated to the younger generation 
- What kind of importance and function the religious faith have for the Vietnamese, especially young people's, self-understanding and integration endeavour facing the society 

2) through this the aim is to create a vantage point for comparative perspectives on the Norwegian religious view, especially considering the Christian faith regarding influence from the modern secular society, and
3) to contribute so that researcher, and others, is going to be a more competent when talking with Vietnamese-Norwegian children, youngsters, parents, voluntaries and professionales regarding questions about upbringing, faith and integration. 

The data material consists of semi-structured interviews.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1995
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income- and Property Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys. This change mainly affected the sample size and sampling issues, with little impact on the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income- and Property Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income- and Property Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key objective is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key objective is to collect income and property data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. Following 1992, a tax system reform was implemented, making it important to gather information to study the effects of the reform, including for the self-employed, and a larger survey on such conditions was integrated specifically. Information is collected on all forms of income, property, tax deductions, and disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews.

Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income- and Property Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings, are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner, is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

title: Infertility in the Gambia, 1994
description: The purpose of this study was to assess the magnitude of infertility in the Gambia and to understand the sociocultural factors that influence coping with and consequences of infertility. The frequency of infertility in rural and urban areas was estimated. Different social and marital variables influencing infertility and subfertility were identified, and infertility levels were compared across different types of backgrounds. Constraints to seeking health care for infertility and the health care system's burden of infertility patients were also investigated. In order to understand the problems of undesired infertility in a country where the desire for children and fertility is very high, a population based estimate of the frequency of sub-/infertility in the Gambia was undertaken. A survey method of assessment was used in a representative random sample of villages. The study also included a review of the problems faced and coping mechanisms employed by infertility clients and types of health care available for infertile couples in the different types and levels of the formal and traditional health system. Primary sterility was found to be fairly uncommon (around 3%), and secondary infertility to be more frequent (around 6%). Primary infertility is a feasible definition for a woman (couple) who have never been pregnant. Secondary infertility implies that the woman has been pregnant, but the result of the pregnancy could be either a live (or dead) child, or an abortion. Half of the infertile couples fail to seek formal health care, and they have to reach a certain level of care in order to be properly managed. Alternative care is often sought. Child fostering is also a frequent solution to childlessness. Childlessness in this context refers to both infertility and childlessness due to loss of children. Child mortality also contributes to childlessness. This is relevant in the Gambia. In-depth interviews revealed that traditional care like marabous are frequently consulted long before formal health care. This delay could be a problem in those cases where infertility is caused by infections. As outlined in the UN 1994 Population Conference in Cairo (ICPD), infertility management should be a part of a complete family planning program. To meet the needs of infertile couples for health care, it is important to strengthen and improve the competence of the formal health sector. In addition, there should be collaboration with, and training of, traditional healers to enable them to identify and refer relevant medical conditions for management at the formal health sector. In order to obtain a sample that would be fairly representative for the whole of the Gambia, the scientific review group developed a sampling frame based on the existing system of relatively equal size Enumeration Areas (EA) that were developed for the Gambia population census 1993, and which is very much up to date. This enables us to have a relatively good statistical base for our estimates. 24 EA's were then randomly drawn for the country as a whole and in order not to oversample the urban area, the randomization was weighted. This means that the results will have to be weighted to give representative results (the weights only account for a very small fraction of the variance). In the selected EAs our field interviewers visited every compound (or households if the compounds consisted of more than one household) and counted the number of people living in each compound or household within compounds.

title: Working conditions at the university - special aspects of the scientific work
description:
This is interview data based on interviews with a total of 20 employees at 5 different departments at the University of Bergen about working conditions at the University.

The purpose of the project was to reconstruct and interpret the similarity and dissimilarity in orientations, language and behavior between academic staff with different academic affiliations at the University of Bergen.

Data were collected through personal interviews between March and July 1994.

title: Microalbuminuria in type II diabetes
description:
The objective of the project is to demonstrate the occurrence of microalbuminuria in a population of type II diabetics who are treated in general practice, and to demonstrate the relationship between microalbuminuria and risk parameters such as hypertension and blood lipid disorders. The sample of patients was taken from three medical centers in Tromsø Municipality. Both interviews and clinical tests have been collected in this study. The dataset represents the clincial tests.

Forty percent of all patients with type I diabetes mellitus develop nephropathy. Nephropathy in this context means albuminuria in the order of magnitude > 200-300 μg/min. Nephropathy in type I diabetics is a marker for other microvascular diseases and involves significantly increased morbidity and mortality over the next 15 years. During the last 10 years, it has become clear that this development can be predicted by the detection of smaller amounts of albumin in the urine, so-called microalbuminuria (20-200 μg/min). Eighty percent of type I diabetics with microalbuminuria develop macroalbuminuria within the next 5-10 years if special therapeutic measures are not taken.

The occurrence and predictive significance of microalbuminuria is more unclear in type II diabetics. Preliminary data from various centers suggest that up to 50% of type II diabetics have microalbuminuria at the time of diagnosis. However, far fewer of these appear to develop diabetic nephropathy. In contrast, the occurrence of microalbuminuria in type II diabetics is strongly associated with increased mortality from other causes, eg cardiovascular death.

This study wants to map the prevalence of microalbuminuria in a sample of type II diabetics, and compare the occurrence of microalbuminuria with the frequency of other possible risk factors such as blood pressure, pathological blood lipids and shifts in metabolic parameters (fructosamine and HbA1C). The hypothesis is that microalbuminuria is a marker of a general risk profile in these patients.

title: Evaluation of Labour Market Training in Vestfold County, 1994
description: The purpose with this study is to get better knowledge of the effect of courses in the context of work and further working training, and to develop criteria to get a better overview and control over the courses/ the quality of the courses. The study was made on an assignment from the county employment office in Vestfold. The sample contained of unemployed people in Vestfold County, drawn in the context of the particapators the working training (AMO). The gross sample was 100, and the net sample was 82. The net sample for all three phases in the data gathering was 65. The participators were followed up in three phases: a interview before the course, a questionnaire in the last week of the course and a telephone interview three months after the course was finished.

title: Lillehammer after the Olympics, 1994
description: The survey aimed to interview persons from the municipality of Lillehammer to examine how they regarded the changes in Lillehammer that had occured in relation to the Olympics. It seeks to observe how the inhabitants in Lillehammer experienced and participated in the Olympic Games in 1994, and how the inhabitants experienced possible aftereffects the event may have caused in the local community.

title: The Norwegian 1994 EU Referendum Study
description: The aim of "The Norwegian 1994 EU Referendum Study" was to study the Norwegian public EU opinion in a broad sense, i.e. to study electoral motives as well as elite participation through organizations such as "Nei til EU", "Europabevegelsen" and "Sosialdemokrater mot EU", as well as the media coverage of the EU issue. In addition, there was a goal to further develop research on referendums. Studies of the 1972 referendum indicated that social position (gender, age, place of residence, education, occupational affiliation and income) was of major importance for voters' views. In addition to the importance of social position, an emphasis was placed on cultural, ideological and political ties and loyalties in the study of the 1994 referendum. The main material for the project was interview data with about 3600 Norwegians. Each respondent was interviewed in advance of the election campaign, during the election campaign and after the vote. The survey was conducted on behalf of the Norwegian University of Science and Technology at the Department of Sociology and Political Science, and coordinated with the Nordic project "Referendum i Europas utkant".

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1993
description:
Statistics Norway has used periodic consumption surveys as a basis for preparing and updating the weight basis for the consumer price index. Detailed overviews of consumption in private households have also formed an important basis for the living conditions statistics, and for research on income, living conditions and e.g. effects of the tax system's use of direct and indirect taxes. The material is also so detailed that it provides an interesting basis for dietary analyses. Data can also be used for demand studies, in economic planning models or for specification of private consumption in the national accounts.

Up to and including 1973, Statistics Norway carried out consumption surveys at intervals of several years, in 1974 Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys, with a somewhat smaller sample. From 1974-1976, data is published for 3 years at a time, and the annual gross sample is approximately 1,500 households, net sample 1,100-1,200 households. The aim was still to build up a detailed overview of consumption in private households, as a basis for updating the weight basis in the consumer price index. One also wants to survey consumption in different types of households. The households are classified according to characteristics such as household type, income, main employee's occupational status and age, area of residence, size of total consumption expenditure, etc. Data is organized in several parts and contains background information about the household (collected through an introductory interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption measured in value and quantity (registered by keeping a diary, respectively for the household and individuals), the household's purchase of durable consumer goods (durable capital goods, collected at a comprehensive closing interview), consumption of self-produced goods and income and tax

Gross sample: 6,518 households for the 3-year period 1992-1994
A household has shared housing and shared meals
Net sample: 1993: 1308 households Somewhat variable for different parts of the data collection
For the 3-year period 1992-1994, the net sample is 4,032 households (Response rate 61.9%)

This is the activist study.

title: Attitudes Towards Immigrants, 1993, NSD Postal Data Collection
description:
Attitudes Towards Immigrants (The Research Council of Norway's coordination survey of 1993).

Many social science research projects that are financed by the Research Council gather data from sample surveys. To better coordinate and take advantage of scientific and economic resources, the Research Council of Norway's coordination committee for survey research was established in January 1983. The committee was to have responsibility for the conduction of surveys, giving researchers the possibility of partaking in joint projects. 

The survey in 1998 was conducted by Statistics Norway as a supplementary survey to the Labour Force Survey of the 1st quarter of 1988. This survey was in turn replicated in 1993, in two parts. Statistics Norway conducted an ordinary survey based on telephone interviews, whilst Norwegian Social Science Data Services conducted a thematically equivalent postal data collection. The data published here stems from the latter, postal survey.

The purpose of these surveys was to observe Norwegians' perceptions and knowledge about immigrants in Norway (except for immigrants from Northern Europe and North America), especially in light of people's social situation and attitudes on other areas. The survey contains information about the work and life situation, contact with immigrants, attitudes towards immigrants and immigration policy, values, political efficacy and economic deprivation. 
 
The purpose of the supplementary survey (the postal part) was to map out to what degree the interview method affects the results of survey research. The questionnaire is thus made as close as possible to the questionnaire in the main survey that was conducted by SSB.

The questions in the beginning of the questionnaire(8-15 in the self-completion form) are simplifies. Question 33-35 in the primary survey are narrowed down to a single question (33 in the SSB survey, given the number 35 in the postal form). In regards to question 40, there are put up different answering alternatives, but this was asked as an open question by SSB. The answering alternatives in question 45 (44 in the postal form) are different. After the printing of the postal forms, "seldom" was included as an answer alternative in the primary sample.

Also, one asks of education in the postal survey. As the dataset contains an education code from SSB's education register, comparisons between stated education and registred education are made possible.

title: Welfare Changes for the Mentally Handicapped Living at Home
description: The project objective was to investigate HVPU reform consequences for handicapped persons' living conditions. The project consisted of the registration of living conditions before and after the implementation of the reform. Personal interviews were conducted with persons close to former residents of institutions (HVPU Committee) in 1989 and 1994 and persons living at home in 1991 and 1994.

title: Labour Force Survey 1994, Year file
description:
Labour Force Survey 1994 - merged year file.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level.  

Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development.  

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics.  

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned.  

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987.  
-------------------------------------------------------------  
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number.  

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.  

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Relationships Between Parents and Children, 1994
description: The aim of the project was to study when children and parents spend time together, in work as well as leisure time, and how children experience this. In those situations where children and parents are together in work and leisure time: who are together with and how do the children experience being together with their parents (their family)? Are the children's emotional concerns satisfied when they are together with their parents (parents and partners)? Questionnaires and interviews were applied among a sample of 5th graders from cities and the country. Gross sample 126, net sample 98.

title: Welfare Changes for the Mentally Handicapped Living at Home, 1994
description: The purpose of this study was to investigate the impact of the HVPU reform on the living conditions of the disabled. The project consisted of recording the living conditions of the mentally handicapped before and after the reform. Personal interviews were conducted with predominantly close relatives of former institutional residents (HVPU committee) in 1989 and 1994 and home residents in 1991. The HVPU committee was 18-67 years old in 1989, and lived in a full-time institution in Sør-Trøndelag, Nord-Trøndelag or Møre og Romsdal. In 1989, the net sample consisted of 591 persons, in 1994 the net sample was 550 persons. The Residential Committee was 0-67 years in 1991, and included all those who did not have full-time employment in the HVPU in 1989-1990. In 1991, the net sample was 716 persons, and in 1994 641 persons.

title: Granted Disability Pension Under Appeal or New Application - Reasons and Justifications, 1994
description: The background for "Granted Disability Pension Under Appeal or New Application - Reasons and Justifications, 1994” was the rising rejection rate in disability pension cases from 1989 to 1993. Yet, a large proportion was retired due to disability, after initial rejection of their application. The project objective was to shed light on the reasons why a significant percentage of the people who had their applications for disability pensions rejected, nevertheless granted disability pensions later on. What percentage of the grants was due to reversal on appeal and what percentage was due to a new application? What were the reasons for reversing the previous decision of rejection? Another important issue is what happened in the period between rejection and grant of a disability pension. In addition, it was also interesting to elucidate the role of the most important processors in the process between refusal and grants for a disability pension. In the project, interviews were conducted with social security officials and doctors. In addition studies of initial refusal cases by document studies were carried out. In the following data set, the data from these document studies are presented.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1994
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Property Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys. This change mainly affected the sample size and sampling issues, with little impact on the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income and Property Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income and Property Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key objective is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key objective is to collect income and wealth data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. Following 1992, a tax system reform was implemented, making it important to gather information to study the effects of the reform, including for the self-employed, and a larger survey on such conditions was integrated specifically. Information is collected on all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, and disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income and Property Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner, is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

title: Attitudes Towards Immigrants, 1993 SSB Interview Data
description:
Attitudes Towards Immigrants (The Research Council of Norway's coordination survey of 1993) SSB Telephone Interview Data.

Many social science research projects that are financed by the Research Council gather data from sample surveys. To better coordinate and take advantage of scientific and economic resources, the Research Council of Norway's coordination committee for survey research was established in January 1983. The committee was to have responsibility for the conduction of surveys, giving researchers the possibility of partaking in joint projects. 

The survey in 1998 was conducted by Statistics Norway as a supplementary survey to the Labour Force Survey of the 1st quarter of 1988. This survey was in turn replicated in 1993, in two parts. Statistics Norway conducted an ordinary survey based on telephone interviews, whilst Norwegian Social Science Data Services conducted a thematically equivalent postal data collection. The data published here stems from the former survey, conducted by SSB.  

The purpose of these surveys was to observe Norwegians' perceptions and knowledge about immigrants in Norway (except for immigrants from Northern Europe and North America), especially in light of people's social situation and attitudes on other areas. The survey contains information about the work and life situation, contact with immigrants, attitudes towards immigrants and immigration policy, values, political efficacy and economic deprivation.

title: Survey of Peoples' Attitudes Towards Their Local Municipality, 1993
description:
This is a project that was carried through by SSB on assignment from the Department of Political Science at the University of Oslo. The aim of the survey was to illuminate the relationships between individuals and their municipality. The municipality is the primary producer of a variety of services that people use on a day to day basis and is thereby an interesting object to study in order to map out relationships between citizens and the government. The survey grasps three sides of individual relations to the municipality - the individual as consumer, payer and citizen. The survey from 1993 is the first of two surveys on this theme. 

The data collection was carried through in two phases, in which the first phase was telephone interviews and the second phase was by postal questionnaires.  

Variables 1-122, except for interview technical variables and register variables, are from the telephone interview part of the survey while variables 123-353 are from the postal part.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1992
description:
Statistics Norway has used periodic consumption surveys as a basis for preparing and updating the weight basis for the consumer price index. Detailed overviews of consumption in private households have also formed an important basis for the living conditions statistics, and for research on income, living conditions and e.g. effects of the tax system's use of direct and indirect taxes. The material is also so detailed that it provides an interesting basis for dietary analyses. Data can also be used for demand studies, in economic planning models or for specification of private consumption in the national accounts.

Up to and including 1973, Statistics Norway carried out consumption surveys at intervals of several years, in 1974 Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys, with a somewhat smaller sample. From 1974-1976, data is published for 3 years at a time, and the annual gross sample is approximately 1,500 households, net sample 1,100-1,200 households. The aim was still to build up a detailed overview of consumption in private households, as a basis for updating the weight basis in the consumer price index. One also wants to survey consumption in different types of households. The households are classified according to characteristics such as household type, income, main employee's occupational status and age, area of residence, size of total consumption expenditure, etc. Data is organized in several parts and contains background information about the household (collected through an introductory interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption measured in value and quantity (registered by keeping a diary, respectively for the household and individuals), the household's purchase of durable consumer goods (durable capital goods, collected at a comprehensive closing interview), consumption of self-produced goods and income and tax

Gross sample: 6,518 households for the 3-year period 1992-1994
A household has shared housing and shared meals
Net sample: 1992: 1,388 households Somewhat variable for different parts of the data collection
For the 3-year period 1992-1994, the net sample is 4,032 households (Response rate 61.9%)

title: Labour Force Survey 1993, Year file
description:
Labour Force Survey 1993 - merged year file.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level.  

Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development.  

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics.  

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned.  

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987.  
-------------------------------------------------------------  
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number.  

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.  

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Substance Abuse in a Society Characterized by Readjustment, 1992-93
description:
This project focus on drug cultures and preventive measures among youth in the local communities affected by the redirection. The aim of the project is to investigate how various conditions in the municipalities can influence 
through preventive measures among the youth. Substance use were studied in four point of intersection: working life, home life, school and measure. The methodical approach of the project: a) intensive field work in the local community, b) in-depth interviews with students, c) survey in the comprehensive school.

title: Beginners Training, 1992
description: The background for the project was to study which effects the new reforms in elementary school has had on teaching- and educational activity during the first years of schooling. One has aimed at saying something about the degree of which central goals for early education actually are met through the comprehension of the individual teacher. The project is divided into three areas: early education, general, Norwegian and math. The survey focuses especially on early education in plans and official documents, influential sources, reading- and writing education, curriculum as a management tool and differences between different types of schools. A postal questionnaire was carried through on a sample of teachers in Møre og Romsdal who were form master in the 1. - 3rd grade, or leader of a service for 6-year olds. Gross sample 593, net sample 254. A couple of in-depth interviews were also carried out.

title: Asthma Among Young Adults in a General Population. Occupational Hazards, Indoor Environment and Diet, 1993
description: The aim of the project was to map out the occurrence of asthma in a general population of young adults in Bergen and its relation to vocational position, indoor climate and diet. The project also included a study where one compared the occurrence and variation of asthma prevalence among young people in Hordaland with the results in 15 other European countries. The hypothesis for the project was that vocational position, indoor climate and diets are in causal connection with variable air current obstruction in Norway. One has therefore aimed at registering such effect variables as air way symptoms, lung infection and usage of medication, as well as registering expositional variables such as smoking habits, vocational exposition, diets, house, indoor climate and allergies. It was a cross sectional study where the effect of expositional levels was followed up in longitudinal studies. Phase one consisted of a questionnaire among 4300 persons, in the second phase 1200 persons were called inn for clinical observations and face-to-face interviewing. The sample consists of a random sample of men and women in the age 20-44 years who are living in Bergen pr. 1. October 1991. The sample was drawn by Sentralkontoret for folkeregistrering. Phase one: gross sample 4300, net sample 3450. Phase two: gross sample 1200, net sample 903.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1993
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Wealth Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys, primarily changing the sample size and sampling issues, with little change to the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income and Wealth Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income and Wealth Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key point is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key point is to collect income and wealth data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. In the years following 1992, a tax system reform was also implemented, making it important to gather information to study the effects of the reform, including for the self-employed, and a larger survey on such conditions was integrated specifically. Information is collected on all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income and Wealth Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

title: National Travel Survey 1991-92, Travels the Day Before the Interview
description:
The "National Travel Survey 1991-92" is the second survey of this series that were conducted in Norway. The preceding survey was conducted in 1985.

The travel survey deals with all types of personal travels: Both daily, shorter travels and longer travels that happen more rarely. Also, use of all sorts of transportation, including walking, are touched upon. The objective is to examine the populations travel activity and travelling pattern. The survey shall, amongst other things, give information about people's travels, how and why they travel and variations in travel activity between different population groups. A further purpose of the survey is to find out how much and how people travel so that the authorities can use this information in connection with planning of the road and public transport sector.

Data on the population's travel activity are vital for national and local transport planning. The results are used as a basis for development of transport models, prognosis work, calculations of exposure in traffic safety work and research. The travel surveys are also important as background for evaluating results from surveys on specific means of transportation or particular geographical areas.

The survey is comprised of five files: person file, short travels, longer travels, household members and cars. This is the short travels file. In this file the units are made up of the journeys conducted by the respondent the day before the interview . Each journey, up to 15 per respondent, makes up one separate unit.

title: National Travel Survey 1991-92, Long Travels
description:
The "National Travel Survey 1991-92" is the second survey of this series that were conducted in Norway. The preceding survey was conducted in 1985.

The travel survey deals with all types of personal travels: Both daily, shorter travels and longer travels that happen more rarely. Also, use of all sorts of transportation, including walking, are touched upon. The objective is to examine the populations travel activity and travelling pattern. The survey shall, amongst other things, give information about people's travels, how and why they travel and variations in travel activity between different population groups. Data on the population's travel activity are vital for national and local transport planning. The results are used as a basis for development of transport models, prognosis work, calculations of exposure in traffic safety work and research. The travel surveys are also important as background for evaluating results from surveys on specific means of transportation or particular geographical areas.

The survey is comprised of five files: person file, short travels, longer travels, household members and cars. This is the longer travels file. In this file, the units are long journeys made by the interviewee in the three-month period prior to the interview. Each journey, up to 8 per interviewee, constitutes a separate unit.

description:

Gross sample: 6330 households for the 3-year period 1989-1991.

Net sample: 1991: 1284 households. Somewhat varying for different parts of the data collection.
For the 3-year period 1989-1991, the net sample is 3,657 households (Response rate 57.8%).

title: The Time Budget Survey, 1990-1991, diary data
description:
The purpose of "The Time Budget Survey 1990-1991" is to gather a comprehensive overview over how the population spends its time on different activities. The Time Budget Surveys are our most important source of information about how much and what types of unpaid work are performed in society, who performs this work, and when it is performed. The Time Budget Surveys also contain data not found in other surveys, e.g., information about circadian rhythms, leisure activities, and time people spend with their children and the rest of the family.

The Time Budget Survey was first carried out in Norway in 1972-1973 and was originally inspired by the international survey "Comparative Time-Budget Projecet", where the same survey program was used in 12 different countries (A. Szalai (red.): The Use of Time, 1965-66). The 1990-91 survet is the third of its kind in Norway and is carried with a view to secure comparability with the results from the international survey.

The data is mainly collected by diaries kept by a selection of the population. In addition, participants are asked to answer questions in a face-to-face interview.

The survey consists of questions about the time use in then following areas:
1. Work
2. Work-related travels
3. Private work; hereunder housework, maintenance, childcare, purchasing and travels
4. Personal needs
5. Education
6. Leisure; hereunder sports and outdoor activities, entertainment, social interaction, media og reading

This dataset contains the data for the interviews. The diaries are documented in a separate file.

title: Fringe Benefits Survey, 1992
description:
The survey deals with fringe benefits in working life, the objective was to map out the extent and distrubution of this type of benefits. The need for the survey was identified by a registration of a one-sided focus on monetary payments in other surveys on income from work.

The data set contains information about demographic and socioeconomic characteristics, features of the work place. payment system, satisfaction with own work, fringe beneftits and attitudes towards fringe benefits. Anonymous information about the respondents' employers can be added to the data set. 

Many social science research projects that are financed by the Research Council of Norway gather data from sample surveys. In order to coordinate and take better advantage of scientific and economic resources, the NAVF coordination committee for survey research was established in January 1983. The committee was to have responsibility for the conduct of interview surveys where different scientists could be part of a joint project. The purpose of the committee was to carry on the tentative work of the surveys of 1984, 1986 and 1988. To avoid big thematical variations between applications from different researchers, a frame for eligible research questions was established. The common theme for the survey in 1992 was "fringe benefits at work place". It came in addition to the annual ISSP-survey in 1992.

title: Changes in the Public Homebased Care
description: The project constitutes a continuation of Kari Wærness' work on home-based care in the late 1970s. The purpose of the project was to gain insight into fundamental processes and changes in home-based care. Information was collected through participant observation and interviews with home helpers, home nurses, and recipients of their services. Information was also gathered through questionnaires answered by home helpers and home nurses in the Bergen district. Based on this information, it is evident that home-based care involves a meeting between a professionalized and institutionalized culture, and a housewife and care culture.

title: Level of Living Among Unemplyed, 1991/1992
description:
In 1991, Statistics Norway conducted a study on living conditions among long-term unemployed. This survey was repeated in 1992, with the same selection, but with a somewhat altered form. The main purpose was to assess the living conditions for long-term unemployed in a state with very high unemployment in society in general, and to compare with other groups. In addition to questions from the general form of the 1991 survey, there was added questions about attitudes to work, the advantages and disadvantages of unemployment, and how unemployment affected the lives and economy, the use of cultural activities and time spent otherwise. The form used in the survey from 1992, any changes in the labor market and economic living conditions where surveyed. One also mapped out the effect for children of the unemployed. The data file is in practice divided into three:

1. The survey in 1991, ordinary study on living conditions of a special committee.

2. Income and wealth data, taken from Income Distribution Survey in 1990.

3. Follow up of the same variety in 1992. Attempts were made to contact 701 of 708 units. It was achieved interviews with 549 of 701 (-152). 48 of these did not give permission to link the 1991 data with the 1992 data, so that a total waiver of the panel is 200

title: Income- and Property Survey 1992
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Property Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys. This change mainly affected the sample size and sampling issues, with little impact on the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income and Wealth Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income and Property Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key objective is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key objective is to collect income and wealth data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. Following 1992, a tax system reform was implemented, making it important to gather information to study the effects of the reform, including for the self-employed, and a larger survey on such conditions was integrated specifically. Information is collected on all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income and Wealth Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner, is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

title: The Time Budget Survey 1990-1991, interview data
description:
The purpose of "The Time Budget Survey 1990-1991" is to gather a comprehensive overview over how the population spends its time on different activities. The Time Budget Surveys are our most important source of information about how much and what types of unpaid work are performed in society, who performs this work, and when it is performed. The Time Budget Surveys also contain data not found in other surveys, e.g., information about circadian rhythms, leisure activities, and time people spend with their children and the rest of the family.

The Time Budget Survey was first carried out in Norway in 1972-1973 and was originally inspired by the international survey "Comparative Time-Budget Projecet", where the same survey program was used in 12 different countries (A. Szalai (red.): The Use of Time, 1965-66). The 1990-91 survet is the third of its kind in Norway and is carried with a view to secure comparability with the results from the international survey.

The data is mainly collected by diaries kept by a selection of the population. In addition, participants are asked to answer questions in a face-to-face interview.

The survey consists of questions about the time use in then following areas:
1. Work
2. Work-related travels
3. Private work; hereunder housework, maintenance, childcare, purchasing and travels
4. Personal needs
5. Education
6. Leisure; hereunder sports and outdoor activities, entertainment, social interaction, media og reading

This dataset contains the data for the interviews. The diaries are documented in a separate file.

title: School Election 1991, Survey Among Pupils
description:
Since 1989, School Elections have been conducted in tandem with parliamentary and municipal elections. The present data file is from the survey conducted in upper secondary schools parallel to the School Election of 1991. In the participating schools, students first vote in the School Election, and subsequently respond to the survey.

In addition to the information concerning voter turnout and centile support for various parties, variables describing the municipality the school is placed in were added. In practice, this entailed adding variables concerning the environment of the school, specifically, information concerning the municipality, such as population density and share of the population that has emigrated from countries outside the Nordic region. The purpose was to facilitate inquiries of the impact of environmental factors in determining the political preferences of the students.

The school election of 1991 was the second in a line of nationally coordinated projects. It started as a loosely formulated idea during the parliamentary election of 1989. IBM expressed the desire to build a framework around the school elections, equivalent to that which they had already developed around parliamentary and municipal elections. NSD – Norwegian Centre for Research Data (now Sikt - Norwegian Agency for Shared Services in Education and Research) wanted to use the schools' ebd-equipment and the statistics program NSDstat to conduct a national election survey of students of upper secondary schools. The two ideas were commonly organized and the project was promised substantial media coverage through NRK's election coverage and text-TV. The project "School Election '89" was a success, and was followed by equivalent projects for subsequent elections. The mock election of 1991 was to all intents and purposes conducted in the same manner as its predecessor. NSD – Norwegian Centre for Research Data and IBM have been the organizers of the school election and the national survey. The project has received valuable support from the Ministry of Church Affairs, Education and Research (KUF). The mock elections were held on the 28th and the 29th of August in most of the participating schools. In total, 410 schools reported election results. This file contains information about each participating school.

title: Children Staying at Shelterrs for Battered Women 1991, Institutional Level Data
description:
The aim of the project was to describe the crisis context that children's situation during residence at a crisis center has to be related to, as well as the residence conditions that the centers offer to children. Further on, one has attempted to illuminate the potential that crisis centers represent as a real service for children who accompany their mothers at these centers. The project consists of an interview survey of 287 women with children at crisis centers, as well as a description of the conditions at the 34 crisis centers. 

There are two datasets where one of them is concerned with data about the children, while the other one includes data about the crisis centers. This file includes the data about the crisis centers.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1990
description:
Statistics Norway has used periodic consumption surveys as a basis for preparing and updating the weight basis for the consumer price index. Detailed overviews of consumption in private households have also formed an important basis for the living conditions statistics, and for research on income, living conditions and e.g. effects of the tax system's use of direct and indirect taxes. The material is also so detailed that it provides an interesting basis for dietary analyses. Data can also be used for demand studies, in economic planning models or for specification of private consumption in the national accounts.

Up to and including 1973, Statistics Norway carried out consumption surveys at intervals of several years, in 1974 Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys, with a somewhat smaller sample. From 1974-1976, data is published for 3 years at a time, and the annual gross sample is approximately 1,500 households, net sample 1,100-1,200 households. The aim was still to build up a detailed overview of consumption in private households, as a basis for updating the weight basis in the consumer price index. One also wants to survey consumption in different types of households. The households are classified according to characteristics such as household type, income, main employee's occupational status and age, area of residence, size of total consumption expenditure, etc. Data is organized in several parts and contains background information about the household (collected through an introductory interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption measured in value and quantity (registered by keeping a diary, respectively for the household and individuals), the household's purchase of durable consumer goods (durable capital goods, collected at a comprehensive closing interview), consumption of self-produced goods and income and tax

Gross sample: 6330 households for the 3-year period 1989-1991.
A household has shared housing and shared meals.
Net sample: 1990: 1201 households. Somewhat variable for different parts of the data collection.
For the 3-year period 1989-1991, the net sample is 3,657 households (Response rate 57.8%).

title: Local Environment Administration in Municipalities with a Narrow Industrial Base, 1991
description: The project looked into the construction of the municipal environment administration and how the local environment actions in six municipalities with a narrow industrial base was made. Bremanger, Sunndal, Sør-Varanger, Vennesla and Årdal. A main goal was to map out the environment policy agenda in the test municipalities. The project included interviews with 110 politicians in the municipalities.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1990-91 panel file, 1.q.1990-2.q.1991
description:
Labour Force Sample Survey 1990-91 panel file, 1.q.1990-2.q.1991.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level.  

Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development.  

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics.  

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned.  

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987.  
-------------------------------------------------------------  
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number.  

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.  

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development.  

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics.  

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned.  

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987.  
-------------------------------------------------------------  
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number.  

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.  

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: The Influence of Kindergarten and Maternal Teaching on Child Schooling, School Grades, Academic Career and Social Adjustment, 1991
description: This survey follows an earlier survey where two groups of children and their mothers, a kinder garden group and a home group, were examined when the children were 7 years old. The survey focuses on the mother's pedagogical style and the child's ability to learn. The following up happened when the children were 20 years old and it included all of the children except of one. Face-to-face interviewing was applied when asking about schooling, working life, social life, well-being and plans for the future. A postal questionnaire was also sent for evaluation of autonomy and Q-sort test for evaluation of self-image.

title: Skill Training in Employment, 1990, Company Sample
description: The project's main goal was to find out how the skill training is organised in different vocations, companies and industries. The variations as well as the common features were of interest, the goal was to limit the factors that can influence successful skill training. Furthermore, the project's aim was to map the experiences the firms and apprentices has with skill training and what to improve and how to do it. The examination was made in two phases. The first sample was the apprentices (491), and the other was the firms (241). In the second phase one went deeply into the factors that were of importance in the first phase. Central actors in the skill training circle were interviewed about skill training questions, motivation, wishes for the future, well being, social relations, the people responsible for the skill training, apprenticeship examination and the relationship between school and workplace.
