title: Income- and Property Survey 1991
description:
Statistics Norway carried out extensive income and property surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984 Statistics Norway switched to yearly surveys, this change mostly effected the sample size and sampling issues and not the type of information that is collected. The information is mostly decided based on publicly available taxation data. The income and property survey data is not a regular sampling survey, they are based on a selection that is drawn from the Tax Administration registries. Therefore, foreign citizens are included if they are part of the National registry, which all individuals with a work and residence permit normally are. Children born during the year are included regardless of date of birth, and the same is true of deceased individuals.

The purpose of the survey is to shine a light on the income of both the whole population and within different groups. One of the main goals is to create statistics about households, households being understood as those who live and eat together, and to create an overview of the distribution of individuals and household based on income size, socioeconomic groups, types of households, geography etc. Another main point is to collect income and property data as background information for the Level of Living and consumer expenditure surveys. Information on all types of income, assets, tax deductions, and disposable income for individuals and households are collected. Some information are gathered from other registers, for example marital status and family composition, while information about household composition is gathered through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than a household, a family can consist of single, unmarried fathers or mothers with children or married couples with or without children. A household consists of all individuals who lives and eats together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows that ca. 90% of all households consists of one family. (see NOS 98/11) Tax free incomes like social benefits, gifts and profits are not included because of the rules of taxation. Rules for percentage taxation of property, valuation of benefits in kind, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc. also create a number of problems.

In addition to tax information, information about education, occupation and industry of individuals are included. For households the composition, type and who the main income earner is, is included. Data is connected from The National Education registry, The Norwegian State Educational Loan Fund, The Housebank, Ministry of Labour and Social Inclusion and National Social Security Administration.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1989
description:
Statistics Norway has used periodic consumption surveys as a basis for preparing and updating the weight basis for the consumer price index. Detailed overviews of consumption in private households have also formed an important basis for the living conditions statistics, and for research on income, living conditions and e.g. effects of the tax system's use of direct and indirect taxes. The material is also so detailed that it provides an interesting basis for dietary analyses. Data can also be used for demand studies, in economic planning models or for specification of private consumption in the national accounts.

Up to and including 1973, Statistics Norway carried out consumption surveys at intervals of several years, in 1974 Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys, with a somewhat smaller sample. From 1974-1976, data is published for 3 years at a time, and the annual gross sample is approximately 1,500 households, net sample 1,100-1,200 households. The aim was still to build up a detailed overview of consumption in private households, as a basis for updating the weight basis in the consumer price index. One also wants to survey consumption in different types of households. The households are classified according to characteristics such as household type, income, main employee's occupational status and age, area of residence, size of total consumption expenditure, etc. Data is organized in several parts and contains background information about the household (collected through an introductory interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption measured in value and quantity (registered by keeping a diary, respectively for the household and individuals), the household's purchase of durable consumer goods (durable capital goods, collected at a comprehensive closing interview), consumption of self-produced goods and income and tax

Gross sample: 6330 households for the 3-year period 1989-1991.
A household has shared housing and shared meals.
Net sample: 1989: 1172 households. Somewhat varying for different parts of the data collection.
For the 3-year period 1989-1991, the net sample is 3,657 households (Response rate 57.8%).

title: Labour Force Survey 1. quarter 1989 - 2. quarter 1990, panel.
description:
Labour Force Survey 1. quarter 1989 - 2. quarter 1990, panel.

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new Norwegian standard classification of occupations (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.   

Every second quarter AKU is extended with a number of questions to collect data accordant to Eurostats / EUs spesifications.

From 1996, the rotation schedule changed, each family now attends eight consecutive quarters. As of 1996 the selection shedule was changed as well with stratification at the county level.

Summary of AKU panels with detailed explanations for panel files:
Data collection for the new rotation plan as the basis for this file is shown below. Participants in the 4th quarter of 1997 is the first batch of 8 possible completed interviews.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Panel files are created by linking eight interviews from an ordinary interview round of the Labour Force Survey. In the table above means that one could connect all the batches (lines) marked X. Participation 4 times used as a criterion for selection (now 8 times).

Annual files and quarterly files consist of respondents who are participating for 1st time, 2nd time, and so on. The weights allow such cross-sections (columns) can be balanced with national figures. If 4 quarters is merged into an annual volume, the quarterly weights be divided by four to attain correct weighted annual numbers.

Each panel (panel files: horisontal in the table) will have a weight compiled for each quarter. Although the weights do not differ much from each other, they will be compiled independently, and each weight should be used only for the data which the quarterly weight is made for. Weighted numbers is valid for one pool, which means ca 12,5 % (1/8) of a total quarterly selection. Therefore, one must multiply by 4 to approximate national quarterly figures

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation.  

This file consists of pool 73 in the table. The weights in the original file were made for monthly use and weights for each quarter are found by dividing the monthly weight on 3.

The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). title: Labour Force Survey 1990, 1st quarter
description:
Labour Force Survey 1990 - 1st quarter

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level.  

Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development.  

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: “Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser”, SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: “Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics.  

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and “over employment” in the original questionnaire were abandoned.  

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The “old” means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the “new” implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987.  
-------------------------------------------------------------  
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable “Labour-market status” was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number.  

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new occupational classification (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.  

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the mentioned areas.

title: Patients who Receive Disablement Benefits due to a Psychiatric Diagnosis, 1990
description: The aim of the project was to gather information about disabled pensioners with a psychiatric diagnosis and why they have retired. The data was gathered by analyzing existing data files at the Norwegian Labor and Welfare Administration and interviewing disability pensioners in the trøndelag counties. NSD has accessible data from the Norwegian Labor and Welfare Administration. The material contains 143 000 disability pensioners and information tied to ca. 600 000 dates. With the exception of existing databases, it is possible to characterize disability pensioners when it comes to socio demographic indicators.

title: Work Load and Muscular Fatigue Among Female Workers in the Fishing Industry, 1990
description:
The aim of the project was to evaluate the burdens and development of work load and muscular fatigue among workers in the fishing industry and assess the variation of the strain parameters that were examined. The units for the survey were 31 female workers in the fishing industry. The workers were interviewed in order to map out the extent of muscular fatigue, production levels, working situation and social conditions. Also, the dataset contains data from a simple clinical study and electromyography measures in order to measure tensions in neck- and shoulder musculature while cleaning and packing - standing or sitting. The participants filled out a visual analog scale before and after the measurements with their own judgment of how tired their neck, shoulder, back, hip and legs felt at various tasks.

The documentation of the dataset is incomplete.

title: Young Adults' Choice of Education and Profession, 1990
description: The project was the Norwegian part of a comparison of 16-20 year old people education and work choices in the Nordic countries. The projects aim was to get new knowledge about young peoples choices of education in the last year of secondary school, the plans for the future when they were in the secondary school and the realisation of the choice 1-2 years after they had finished secondary school. In 1987 a questionnaire was given to 620 young people in 25 9th grades in ten schools in the Bergen area.  The investigation was repeated in 1988 and 1989. In 1989 there were also carried through interviews with ca. 80 pupils who did not to as the most common to start with high school after secondary school. A follow-up questionnaire was sent out in 1990 to 520 pupils to map out what they did to years after they finished 9th grade.

title: Models for Development of Dental Diseases, 1990
description: The project contains a survey concerning health, environment and development in a birth cohort of children and their families. A subproject concerning dental health and dental health behavior was also included. The sample selected was all children born at Kongsvinger hospital in a 16-month period spanning from 1979 to 1980. The data material consists of two data files. The largest data file is based on interviews of parents on the maternity ward after birth, questionnaires completed by the public health nurse at the 6-month control, questionnaires completed by the dental nurse at the 18-month control, questionnaires completed by a dentist at the 3-year control, questionnaires completed by the children's parents at the 7-year control at the dental clinic, questionnaires to children and parents at the 10-year control at the dental clinic in addition to reports from the clinical and radiological examinations. The second data file contains data from clinical examinations and radiological teething rings at the 7-year control at the dentists. The material consists of a birth cohort of 231 children born in 1979/1980. At the survey in 1979/1980 the data material consisted of 231 families, in 1982 it consisted of 201 families, in 1987 it consisted of 133 families, and in 1990 it consisted of 107 families.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 4.quarter 1988 - 1.quarter1990, panel file
description:
This dataset is based on the panel survey "Labour Force Survey, 4. quarter 1988 - 1. quarter 1990, panel file".
The Labour Force Survey will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. The Labour Force Survey has evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1996, the LFS questionnaire, file description, and standard coding of industry and occupation was significaly revised. Data collection eventually went over to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. The present file has incorporated these changes. New industrial classification in this file. (NOS C 182, based on EU standard NACE, Rev.1) New Classification of Occupations (STRYK) based on ISCO 88 is adopted from 1996, in these data the occupation are coded for new standard.

From 1996 the rotation schedule changed, so every family now is attending eight consecutive quarters. From 1996, also the sampling plan changed, making stratification at the county level. This file covers six quarters.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1990
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Property Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys. This change mainly affected the sample size and sampling issues, with little impact on the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income and Wealth Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income and Property Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key objective is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key objective is to collect income and wealth data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. Information is collected on all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income and Wealth Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner, is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

The panel is made by linking four interviews from an ordinary interview round in AKU. In the table above it means that one could link all the groups (lines) marked X. The 4th participation is used as a criterion to select. In 1975 there was a rupture in the panel systematic, and one returned to the original six-quarter rotation plan, which has been stable since then. 
In 1975 there was a rupture in the systematic of the panel, see 1975. In the  labour force surveys (AKU) 1976 it was taken into use a slightly modified questionnaire and one returned to the original six-quarter rotation plan, which has been stable since.

Looking at a particular column, for example at 1st quarter 1988, one can see that 1/4 of the IO is responding for the 4th time, 3rd time and so on. Together they sum up the total of one quarter. If one takes 4 quarters and sums them up annually the quarter weight must be divided by 4 to give the correct weighted figures for the annual volume. 

The panel goes in the other direction (horizontal) and each panel will have 4 weights, one for each quarter. Although they do not differ much they will be prepared independently and each weight should be used only for data from the quarterly the weight is made. Weighted numbers is for one group, circa 25% of the total quarterly sample, and one must therefore multiply by 4 to get the approximately national quarterly. 
With "approximate national quarterly" means that since the panel linking involves a certain selection process, so it may cause minor differences from the weighted national numbers one gets for each quarter. 

This panel includes pool 69 in the table. The weights in the original panel is prepared to be used as monthly quarter weights by dividing the monthly quarter weight by 3. It appears that there have been problems with the link to the 2nd quarter 1988. The share of IO links constitute only circa 50% of the normal sample size in this quarter: 

Valid N in the panel: 2592 - 1351 - 2723 - 2670

Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988 there was a change in AKU which resulted in a new file description, and also the status code for the status on the labor market changed. The present file overlap with this change, but still follows essentially the file description that was prepared for the change. This has practical reasons, the nine panels that has the last interview from the 1st quarter 1987 to 1st quarter 1989 was prepared and transferred simultaneous from SSB to NSD. However, it is made two versions of these panels: because V009, V036 and V073 (status labor market) changed, data is also encoded to obtain comparable data before and after the adjustments between 1st and 2nd quarter 1988. 
NSD0605 provides data with original codes. NSD0606 provides encoded data. This file therefore have the codes for the variables V009,V036 and V073  who are direct comparable with NSD603 and NSD0601.
This file (NSD0608_2 is a recoded panel).

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1988
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1988" is conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the third of the panel period between 1986-1988.

title: Labour Force Survey 1987-89 panel file, 4th quarter 1987-1st quarter 1989
description:
As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). Looking at a particular column, for example at 1st quarter 1988, one can see that 1/4 of the IO is responding for the 4th time, 3rd time and so on. Together they sum up the total of one quarter. The panel goes in the other direction (horizontal) and each panel will have 4 weights, one for each quarter. This has practical reasons, the nine panels that has the last interview from the 1st quarter 1987 to 1st quarter 1989 was prepared and transferred simultaneous from SSB to NSD. However, it is made two versions of these panels: because V009, V036 and V073 (status labor market) changed, data is also encoded to obtain comparable data before and after the adjustments between 1st and 2nd quarter 1988. 
NSD0605 provides data with original codes. NSD0606 provides encoded data. This file therefore have the codes for the variables V009,V036 and V073  who are direct comparable with NSD603 and NSD0601.

SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) as of Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others knowledge about the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS respondents are interviewed two consecutive quarters, then after two more quarters they are interviewed again in two consecutive quarters. Labour Force Survey 1972 is the first complete LFS year long survey. Originally it was intended that such statistics would be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the insurance fund membership files. title: Medicines and Children. Knowledge, Attitudes and Counselling Among Pharmacy Personnel, 1989
description: This survey is the third part of a larger project about medicine usage among children. The first two parts gathered data about prescription of medicine for children in the age 0-12 years in the whole country, and the parents experience and attitudes. The aim of the survey was to gather information about knowledge, attitudes and counselling with regard to children's medicine usage, especially in connection with fever. 60 pharmacists and 60 druggists were interviewed by telephone or face-to-face interviewing. The questions addressed the general level of knowledge about fever and temperature measurements among children and adults, and guidance for parents at pharmacies. Questions were also asked about ones own medicine usage.

title: Service Production with Basis in the Fishing Industry, 1989.
description: The purpose of this project was to investigate the effects of delivery with regard to goods and services to the fishing industry. The survey was carried out through the creation of a business database sorted by activity on the user side, location etc. In addition, interviews with users of fisheries services were carried out.

title: Epidemiology of Asymptomatic Carriage of Neisseria Meningitidis in Norway, 1989
description: The aim of the project was to study the extensiveness and causes of carrier conditions among the pathogens Neisseria meningitides clones. It was carried out personal interviews and postal questionnaires among children and youth in the age 7-20 years in Oslo, Bergen, Trondheim, Stavanger and Kristiansand, all together 946 persons.

title: XML Data Documentation for Working Conditions 1989
description:
In 1989, Statistics Norway conducted an extensive survey that examined various dimensions of working life, including the work environment, wages, labor union activities, negotiations, job satisfaction, and attitudes toward work. A key objective of the survey was to capture employees' perceptions of their workplace in the context of the broader organizational environment. To achieve this, both company leaders and union representatives were interviewed, and supplementary data, such as wage levels and hiring policies, were collected.

The data is available at the following levels: company, employee, general manager, HR manager, and union representative. Statistics Norway (SSB) and the Institute for Social Research (ISF) were responsible for initiating, designing, and conducting the survey.

title: Occupational Record of Petroleum Workers, 1987
description:
The data set is taken from “Occupational Record of Petroleum Workers” as Rogalandsforskning conducted in 1987.

The project's purpose was to obtain knowledge on the individual level the relationship between the oil workers' background and professional career. The question was about the oil business stand out from other activities in terms of income and career opportunities for different groups. To answer this, the 731 people interviewed. 

Compared with other activities characterized the oil business by: Great salary and career benefits; large internal wage differentiation; in the offshore business is little formal education compensated with house training and experience that provide good wages and career opportunities; very large gender inequalities - both onshore and offshore; particularly good opportunities for the operators attract workers from other parts - especially from engineering, but also from the drilling and services companies; negligible mobility out of the oil business, and small transition from offshore to onshore; very little geographic mobility among offshore employees.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1989
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Property Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys. This change mainly affected the sample size and sampling issues, with little impact on the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income and Wealth Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income and Property Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key objective is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key objective is to collect income and wealth data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. Information is collected on all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income and Wealth Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner, is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

title: Research Diffusion and Journalism, 1988
description: Journalists are important communicators of scientific research data to the general public as well as public officials and private actors who directly benefit from its use. The form and content of this type of journalism has considerable influence on what information is made available to the users. Consequently, a systematic overview of the factors affecting this type of journalism is important. The objective of this project was to expand the knowledge of researchers and journalists concerning research data in the mainstream media, as well as developing ideas to facilitate communication between them. In addition, an educational program was developed for the Norwegian Journalism School. The project was conducted using a content analysis of a sample of articles concerning scientific research from daily newspapers. Researchers and journalists were then contacted and questioned about the article's content and their experience with journalistic coverage of scientific research. The respondents were 249 scientists and 109 journalists. The sample consisted of scientists interviewed in 13 Norwegian daily newspapers, and the journalists who interviewed them. The response rate was 77 percent.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1987
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1987" is conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the second of the panel period between 1986-1988.

title: Electrostimulation of the Pelvic Floor in Female Urinary Incontinence, 1988
description:
The aim of the survey was to develop new treatment methods for women with urge incontinence such as electrical stimulation of the pelvic floor and by applying the treatment on women with different forms of functional disturbance in the lower urinarias. The data material were gathered by interview and clinical surveys of three groups of women (all together 269 persons) at Regionsykehuset i Trondheim.

The documentation of the data is incomplete.

title: Young Disabled Adults: Living and Working Conditions, Social Isolation, Quality of LifeName, 1988
description: The survey aimed to shed light on the relationship between living and working conditions for young adults with physical disabilities on one hand, and social isolation and quality of life on the other. Questionnaire data were collected from the Health Survey in Nord-Trøndelag, which covered, among other things, disability, living and working conditions, and quality of life, i.e., subjectively experienced and self-reported satisfaction and social isolation. The second phase of the survey included more extensive interviews with a sample of disabled individuals. These data provide thorough information on the aspects intended to be illuminated, including perceived opportunities and desires for employment and living conditions, as well as experienced barriers, isolation, and exclusion. The interview survey was conducted among disabled individuals aged 20-30, residing in Nord-Trøndelag, and receiving disability benefits and/or basic benefits and/or attendance benefits as of January 1, 1986. Of the 195 individuals interviewed, 106 participated in the Health Survey. A linked dataset from these two sources has been prepared.

title: Labour Force Survey 2nd quarter 1987 - 3rd quarter 1988, panel file, recoded
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others knowledge about the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS respondents are interviewed two consecutive quarters, then after two more quarters they are interviewed again in two consecutive quarters. Labour Force Survey 1972 is the first complete LFS year long survey. Originally it was intended that such statistics would be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

General information on the Labour Force Survey: http://www.nsd.uib.no/data/ny_individ/norStudy/aku.html

Panel files are created by linking four interviews from an ordinary interview round in LFS. In the table above this means that one could connect all the groups (lines) marked X.
Group 1 is removed because the intweviews started 4th quarter 1971.
Participation 4 times is used as a selection criterion, therefore everyone that didn't participate in the 4th interview has been omitted.
In 1975 SSB changed selection strategy. The groups that were left out due to this (started in 1973 or 1974, should've had one or more interviews in 1975) are removed.

Looking at a particular column, for example. Q1 1983, we will see that 1/4 of respondents are included for the 4th time, the 3rd time etc. The total sum of the material for one quarter. Quarterly weights allows one such block (column) to be balanced with national figures. If taking 4 groups and turning them into a year file the weights must be divided by 4 to give proper weighted figures.

Panel files goes in the other direction (horizontally) and each panel will have four weights, one for each quarter, and although they do not differ much will they be prepared independently and each weight should be used only for data from the quarterly weight is made. Weighted figures are for a pool, ie ca. 25% of the total quarterly sample and must therefore multiply by 4 to get approximately the national quarterly.

With "almost national quarterly" means that since panel connectors innebærerer a certain selection, as it may cause minor differences from the weighted national figures one gets for each quarter
 
This panel file covers group 66 in the table above. The file itself is created later, based on a file description from 1988; the weights in the original file is created to be used on months.

Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988 there was a reorganization of the LFS including resulted in a new file description, and that the status codes for status in the labor market changed. The present panel file is located exactly at the forefront of this change, but still follows essentially the file description that was prepared for the change. This has practical reasons, the 9 panel files have the latest interview from Q1 1987 to Q1 1989 was compiled together and transmitted simultaneously from SSB to NSD. However, it created two versions of these panel files: Because the V009, V036 and V073 (status on the labor market) is changed, the data also recoded to obtain comparable data before and after the adjustments between the 1st and 2nd quarter 1988
NSD0603 provides data with original codes, NSD0604 provide encoded data, the entire 1987 and 1988 codes that are comparable with subsequent encoding.

title: Battered Women - Reproductive Consequenses, 1988
description: The purpose of the project was to establish occurrence of violence against women and examine reproductive consequences; how gynecological disorders and adverse pregnancy outcomes are related to violence. The data collection was carried out as a personal interview where questions were raised about conditions, care work, education, housing, economics, religion, physical and mental illness history, physician contact, medical certificates, discounted functions, pregnancy / childbirth, contraception use, gynecological diseases, severe life events and sexual experiences. Various questionnaires were also used to map health: a questionnaire concerning ailments the past two weeks, the General Health Questionnaire, General Well Being Schedule, network map, a questionnaire concerning conflict mapping and a questionnaire concerning ailments for people who have been subjected to great stress.

title: Attitudes towards Environmental Issues, 1988
description:
Based on the report from the UN Commission for Environment and Development, the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) initiated the world-wide interview survey on environment and environmental protection the fall of 1987. UNEP saw it as important to analyze data on people's attitudes towards environment, environmental protection and nature resources in different parts of the world.

In the survey's introductory letter, UNEP underlines that " such a study may help the UN organizations to structure their programmes, the multilateral financial institutions to identify where possibilities exist, bilateral aid agencies to target their funds properly and national governments of countries tested to reorient their development activities". In total, 21 countries participated in the survey.

The Norwegian survey was firstly suggested by the Ministry of Environment and finances by the Research Council of Norway. Norwegian Social Science Data Services (NSD) were assigned the task of project coordination. Norwegian Gallup Institute collected the data.

title: Some Aspects of Urinary Incontinence with Patients of General Practictitoners, 1988
description:
The aim of the project was to illuminate some aspects of urge incontinence in general practise and aspects of being affected by urge incontinence, either one has been in contact with health services of not. The focus was set on self-care, contact patterns in general practise, patient satisfaction in general practise, living with urge incontinence, progress, patient's delay, usage of health services, determinants of contact with physician etc. Participants were recruited by the patient index files of Saba Mølnycke A/S. The net sample was 337 persons and interviews were conducted by postal questionnaires, telephone interviews and face-to-face interviews. 

The documentation of the dataset is incomplete.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1988
description:
Statistics Norway carried out extensive income and property surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984 Statistics Norway switched to yearly surveys, this change mostly effected the sample size and sampling issues and not the type of information that is collected. The information is mostly decided based on publicly available taxation data. The income and property survey data is not a regular sampling survey, they are based on a selection that is drawn from the Tax Administration registries. Therefore, foreign citizens are included if they are part of the National registry, which all individuals with a work and residence permit normally are. Children born during the year are included regardless of date of birth, and the same is true of deceased individuals.

The purpose of the survey is to shine a light on the income of both the whole population and within different groups. One of the main goals is to create statistics about households, households being understood as those who live and eat together, and to create an overview of the distribution of individuals and household based on income size, socioeconomic groups, types of households, geography etc. Another main point is to collect income and property data as background information for the Level of Living and consumer expenditure surveys. Information on all types of income, assets, tax deductions, and disposable income for individuals and households are collected. Some information are gathered from other registers, for example marital status and family composition, while information about household composition is gathered through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than a household, a family can consist of single, unmarried fathers or mothers with children or married couples with or without children. A household consists of all individuals who lives and eats together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows that ca. 90% of all households consists of one family. (see NOS 98/11) Tax free incomes like social benefits, gifts and profits are not included because of the rules of taxation. Rules for percentage taxation of property, valuation of benefits in kind, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc. also create a number of problems.

In addition to tax information, information about education, occupation and industry of individuals are included. For households the composition, type and who the main income earner is, is included. Data is connected from The National Education registry, The Norwegian State Educational Loan Fund, The Housebank, Ministry of Labour and Social Inclusion and National Social Security Administration.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1987, 4.quarter
description:
Statistics Norway (SSB) has conducted official quarterly Labor Force Surveys (LFS) since the first quarter of 1972. The purpose is to provide labor market authorities and other stakeholders with insights into the occupational structure of the population and its development over time. The surveys aim to provide a foundation and statistical material for occupational forecasts and labor market research. In the LFS, a respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, followed by a two-quarter break, and is then interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample size was approximately 10,000–11,000 respondents each quarter until 1988. The 1976 Labor Force Survey represents the fifth complete annual series of the LFS. Initially, the LFS was intended to serve as an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics, which were based on the membership records of social insurance offices. However, the insurance-based statistics were discontinued when sickness insurance was integrated into the National Insurance Scheme (Folketrygden) as of January 1, 1971. Over time, the LFS has evolved into the most important source of knowledge about the state and development of the labor market.

In 1975, Statistics Norway revised the sampling plan for interview surveys. See, for example, Art.37: Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser, SSB (Steinar Tamsfoss), and SØS 33: Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelser (Ib Thomsen). At the same time, the estimation method for extrapolating data to national figures was changed, enabling the production of reliable regional statistics as of 1975. The 1975 revisions also led to changes in how interviews were conducted in batches, which, among other things, resulted in a break in the panel systematics of the LFS.

For the 1976 Labor Force Survey, a slightly revised questionnaire was introduced, and the original six-quarter rotation plan was reinstated. The new questionnaire allowed for better identification of individuals in the categories of family workers and those temporarily absent from income-generating work. This adjustment led to approximately 30,000–35,000 more individuals being classified as employed. Additionally, the category of job seekers without income was expanded to include those involuntarily laid off. Questions related to underemployment and overemployment from the original questionnaire were removed.

From the first quarter of 1987, the estimation method (extrapolation to national figures) was slightly modified, and a minor adjustment to the definition of employment was made. To ensure comparability over time, new versions of the LFS data files were created retroactively to 1980. As a result, two versions of the files exist from 1980 onwards: one using the "old" estimation method and the other the "new" method. The "old" version is comparable with the originally published figures for the period 1972–1987, while the "new" version ensures that data from 1980–1987 are comparable with figures published after 1987.

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: NSD1450
description: The purpose of the project was to study wage negotiations when the state is a party. The focus was on the negotiation system importance for the negotiation behaviour and the outcome. Special emphasis on mapping out the states double role as a coordinate party and superior body in the pay settlement. Economic statistic materials, document studies and interview materials were used in the project. One has looked into normative structures and on which arguments and criteria the elites think is most important and most effective in connection with deciding the pay. The prepared material included interview data with 242 interview objects.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1986
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1986" is conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the first of the panel period between 1986-1988.

title: The National Broadcasting System's User Survey - Ocober 1987
description:
The dataset is from the 'Survey on Radio Listening and Television Viewing - September 1987', conducted by Statistics Norway (SSB) on behalf of the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation (NRK). The June 1987 survey was part of SSB's listener and viewer surveys for NRK. From 1967 to 1977, SSB conducted annual general radio and television surveys for NRK. Between 1978 and 1985, several special surveys were conducted, with general surveys only carried out in 1980 and 1983. Since 1985, a new arrangement with three general surveys per year has been in place. This change was due to the new media situation in the 1980s, with the increasing availability of multiple channels in both radio and television.

The main objectives of the new survey arrangement were to collect data on:
- Changes in listening and viewing patterns over time
- Variations in listening and viewing patterns in different seasons
- Use and choice of various channels and programs in radio and television
- How people listen to the radio and watch television
- Access to radio and television sets and equipment used with these devices

In addition, NRK wanted to ask questions about people's opinions on various aspects of NRK's programming and specific individual programs or types of programs. The aim was to conduct surveys that could provide a basis for NRK's work in creating a program offering that listeners and viewers would benefit from, and that could help illuminate the role of radio and television in people's lives. The survey also includes questions about respondents' interest in various types of information and their satisfaction with the mass media's coverage of different topics.

Three general surveys were conducted in 1987, in February, June, and September/October. This file contains data from the September survey.

A gross sample of approximately 1,500 individuals was drawn. These individuals were interviewed in 7 batches about their listening and viewing habits the day before and two days before the interview. The net sample was 1,178 individuals.

For mapping time use, the day was divided into fixed 10-minute time intervals. Activities per time unit were recorded, whether listening to the radio, watching TV, or doing other things. This allows the two days to be "stacked" on each other; to study time use, one individual can be represented with two days. Therefore, each person is included twice in the dataset, so the number of units (days) is twice the number of respondents. This was done to include the individual's responses for both program days in the same variable. For example, the variable 'v162 Listened to radio 06.05 - CHANNEL CODE' contains information about what the individual listened to on the radio at 06.05 for both days, yesterday and the day before yesterday.

title: Foreign Adopted Children in Norway. Adaptability at the Age of 12, 1987
description: The survey's purpose was to investigate how children from the third world, who have been adopted to Norway, adapt to their new family and school. The starting point was that adopted children are a group that has experienced dramatic changes in their childhood circumstances, and their adaption will illuminate potentials for improvement. 151 adopted children born in 1975/1976 and their families were interviewed. Also, the child's teacher answered a questionnaire. A control group of children in the same age were also interviewed. Adaption was measured by applying Rutter's scale. The variables are concerned with the child's behavior at school and home, the child's development, special problems, school situation, language, intelligence, well-being and social skills.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1987
description:
Statistics Norway carried out extensive income and property surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984 Statistics Norway switched to yearly surveys, this change mostly effected the sample size and sampling issues and not the type of information that is collected. The information is mostly decided based on publicly available taxation data. The income and property survey data is not a regular sampling survey, they are based on a selection that is drawn from the Tax Administration registries. Therefore, foreign citizens are included if they are part of the National registry, which all individuals with a work and residence permit normally are. Children born during the year are included regardless of date of birth, and the same is true of deceased individuals.

The purpose of the survey is to shine a light on the income of both the whole population and within different groups. One of the main goals is to create statistics about households, households being understood as those who live and eat together, and to create an overview of the distribution of individuals and household based on income size, socioeconomic groups, types of households, geography etc. Another main point is to collect income and property data as background information for the Level of Living and consumer expenditure surveys. Information on all types of income, assets, tax deductions, and disposable income for individuals and households. Some information are gathered from other registers, for example marital status and family composition, while information about household composition is gathered through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than a household, a family can consist of single, unmarried fathers or mothers with children or married couples with or without children. A household consists of all individuals who lives and eats together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows that ca. 90% of all households consists of one family. (see NOS 98/11) Tax free incomes like social benefits, gifts and profits are not included because of the rules of taxation. Rules for percentage taxation of property, valuation of benefits in kind, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc. also create a number of problems. In addition to tax information, information about education, occupation and industry of individuals are included. For households the composition, type and who the main income earner is, is included. Data from The National Education registry, The Norwegian State Educational Loan Fund, The Housebank, Ministry of Labour and Social Inclusion and National Social Security Administration.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1986, 4.quarter
description:
Statistics norway has conducted official quarterly Labor Force Surveys (LFS) since the first quarter of 1972. The purpose of these surveys is to provide labor market authorities and other stakeholders with insights into the occupational structure of the population and its development over time. The surveys aim to provide a basis and statistical material for occupational forecasts and labor market research. In the LFS, each respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, followed by a two-quarter break, and then re-interviewed for another two consecutive quarters. The sample size was approximately 10,000–11,000 respondents per quarter until 1988. The 1976 Labor Force Survey marks the fifth complete year of data collection. Originally, the Labour Force Survey was intended as an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the social insurance membership registry. However, the registry-based statistics were discontinued when the sickness benefits system was incorporated into the National Insurance Scheme on January 1, 1971. Over time, the LFS has evolved into the primary source of information on labor market conditions and developments.

In 1975, Statistics norway revised its sampling plan for interview-based surveys. Refer to Article 37: On the Use of Samples at the Interview Surveys Office, SSB (Steinar Tamsfoss) and SØS 33: Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's Sample Surveys (Ib Thomsen) for details. Concurrently, the estimation method for scaling up to national figures was modified, enabling reliable regional data from 1975 onwards. This adjustment led to changes in how respondents were interviewed in batches, creating a discontinuity in the panel methodology of the LFS.

During the 1976 Labor Force Survey, a slightly revised questionnaire was introduced, and the original six-quarter rotation plan was reinstated. The updated questionnaire improved the identification of individuals classified as family workers and those temporarily absent from income-generating work, resulting in approximately 30,000–35,000 additional individuals being classified as employed. The category of job seekers without income was also expanded to include those involuntarily laid off. However, questions regarding underemployment and overemployment from the original questionnaire were removed.

Starting in the first quarter of 1987, the estimation method (scaling to national figures) was further refined, and a minor adjustment was made to the definition of employment. To ensure comparability over time, new versions of the LFS files were created for the period starting in 1980. Consequently, there are two versions of files from 1980 onward: one with the "old" estimation method and another with the "new" method. The "old" version ensures comparability with the originally published figures for 1972–1987, while the "new" version aligns data for the 1980–1987 period with the figures published after 1987.

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1986, 3.quarter
description:
Statistics Norway (SSB) has conducted official quarterly Labor Force Surveys (LFS) since the first quarter of 1972. The purpose of these surveys is to provide labor market authorities and other stakeholders with insights into the occupational structure of the population and its development over time. The surveys aim to serve as a basis for labor market forecasts and research. In the LFS, respondents are interviewed in two consecutive quarters, followed by a two-quarter break, and then interviewed again in two consecutive quarters. The sample size was approximately 10,000–11,000 respondents each quarter until 1988. The 1976 Labor Force Survey represents the fifth complete annual dataset of the LFS. Initially, the LFS was intended to serve as an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the membership records of the national social insurance offices. However, these records ceased to be used for statistical purposes when sickness benefits were integrated into the National Insurance Scheme (Folketrygden) on January 1, 1971. Over time, the LFS has evolved into the most important source of information on the labor market's status and trends.

In 1975, Statistics Norway revised its sampling framework for interview-based surveys. For further details, see Article 37: *On the Use of Sampling at the Office for Interview Surveys* (Steinar Tamsfoss, SSB) and SØS 33: *Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's Sample Surveys* (Ib Thomsen). At the same time, the estimation method for projecting national figures was modified, enabling reliable regional data from 1975 onward. This revision also altered the interview scheduling, resulting in changes to the LFS’s panel system.

The 1976 Labor Force Survey introduced a slightly revised questionnaire and reverted to the original six-quarter rotation plan. The updated questionnaire improved the identification of individuals categorized as family workers and those temporarily absent from income-generating work, which led to an additional 30,000–35,000 individuals being classified as employed. The category of job seekers without income was also expanded to include individuals on involuntary temporary layoff. Questions regarding underemployment and overemployment were removed from the original questionnaire.

Starting in the first quarter of 1987, the estimation method (used to project national figures) was slightly adjusted, and a minor change was made to the definition of employment. To ensure comparability over time, new versions of the LFS datasets were created retroactively to 1980. As a result, two versions of the datasets exist for the period from 1980 onward: one using the "old" estimation method and one using the "new" method. The "old" version is comparable with the originally published figures for 1972–1987, while the "new" version provides data for 1980–1987 that aligns with figures published after 1987.

This file is based on the *new* estimation method.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1985
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1985" is the 12th annual consumer survey conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the third, and last, of the panel period between 1983-1985.

title: Labour Force Survey 2. quarter 1985 - 3. quarter 1986, panel
description:
Labour Force Survey, 2. quarter 1985 - 3. quarter 1986, panel (6 quarters).

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (LFS). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research.  

In the Labour Force Surveys each respondent is interviewed for two subsequent quarters, followed up by a two quarter break. Then they are again followed up with two subsequent quarters.
The first complete edition of LFS is the Labour Force Survey of 1972. Such statistics was originally intended to be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics which were based on the National Insurance membership index. The statistics was based on the national insuranse index until the sickness benefit was included in the national insuranse system januar 1st 1971. Gradually LFS has evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the condition and trends in the labor market.

General information on the Labour Force Surveys: http://www.nsd.uib.no/data/ny_individ/norStudy/aku.html

Panel files are created by linking four interviews from an ordinary interview round of the LFS. In the table above this means one could connect all the pools (lines) labeled X. Participation 4th time is used as the criterion for the selection process.
In 1975 there was a break in the panel systematics, see 1975. The Labour Force Survey of 1976, adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and you came back to the original 6-quarterly rotation schedule which have been stable since.

If one looks at a particular column, for example 1st quarter of 1984, we will see that 1/4 of the respondents are included for the fourth time, for the third time, etc. The total sums the the material for one block. Quarterly weighting allows one such block (column) to be balanced with national figures. To give proper weighted figures for a annual volume consisting of four quarters, the quarterly weights has to be divides by 4.

The panels goes horizontally and each panel will have 4 weights, one for each quarter. Although they do not differ much, the weights will be prepared independently and each of them should be used for the data which the quarterly weight is made. Weighted figures apply to a pool, which means approx. 25% of the total quarterly sample, and one has to multiply by 4 to get the approximate national quarterly figures.

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation. 

This panel consider pool 58 in the table above.

title: Income- and Property Statistics, 1986, household level data
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Property Statistics in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984 Statistics Norway went over to a system of annual surveys, the change was mainly on sample size and selection issues, and little of what information is collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. Income surveys are not considered as a regular sample survey, they are based on a sample drawn from the tax agency's records. This means that foreign nationals are included if they are registered in the Central Register, and they will normally be if they have work and residence permit. Children born during the year is included regardless of date of birth and the same goes for people who died during the year.

The purpose of the  Income and Property Statistics has been to assess the income situation for the whole population and for different groups. A main point is to generate statistics on cost households, i.e. households who live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of persons and households by income size, socio-economic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another main point is to collect income and wealth data as background forthe  Income and Property Statistics. In the years after 1992, after the reform of the rax system, it was considered important to gather information in order to study the effects of the reform, also for self-employed, and a larger syrvey was integrated of such matters in particular. Information on all forms of income, wealth, tax, disposable income for individuals and households are collected. Some information is linked from other registers, for example information on marital status and family composition, while information on household composition are obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower term than household, a family may consist of single, unmarried father or mother with children, or married couples with or without children. A household on the other hand, includes all  people who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that approximately 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notater 98/11 SSB 1998: Inntekts- og formuesundersøkelsen 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a distinctly social character, gifts and prizes fall outside due to tax rules. Rules for the percent of property tax assessment, valuation of benefits in kind, personal withdrawals, depreciation etc, also creates some problems. In addition to the pure tax information information on such as education, occupation and income for individuals and for the household: the composition, type and who is the main income earner is invluded. This data is linked to, among else, data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, Housing, Social Affairs (social assistance) and the National Insurance Administration.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1985, 4th quarter, new estimation factor
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others familiar with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS one respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, then there's a break for two quarters and then the respondent is interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10 to 11,000 respondents each quarter until the 1988. The  Labour Force Survey 1976 is the fifth full year with LFS. Originally it was intended that the LFS should be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics that were based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1975 SSB changed the selection plan for surveys, see respectively Art.37: On the use of random samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss) and SES 33: Principles and Methods of Statistics Norway samples (Ib Thomsen) Meanwhile, the estimation method for inflating national figures changed so that there are reasonable numbers for regions from the 1975 reorganization in 1975 led to interviews changing in how they were performed in the pools before and after. This meant, among other things a break from the Panel system used in the LFS.

By Labour Force Survey 1976, it was adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and we came back on the original six-quarter rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group and family workers temporarily absent from work were identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. Group seekers without work income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions of subordinates and employment in the original form was deleted

From Q1 1987 the estimation method (inflating the national figures) slightly changed, moreover, there was a minor adjustment to the definition of employment. To achieve comparability backwards in time was therefore developed new versions of LFS files back to 1980 From 1980 there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with the "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable to the original published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means of data for the period 1980-1987 which utilizes this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with the figures published after 1987

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.
