title: The National Broadcasting System's User Survey, June 1985
description:
This dataset is derived  from the “The National Broadcasting System's User Survey, June 1985” that was conducted by Statistics Norway (SSB) on assignment from the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation (NRK). The survey from June 1985 was included as a part of Statistics Norway's listener -and viewer surveys for the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation. Statistics Norway conducted from 1967 to 1977 annual general radio and television surveys on behalf of the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation (NRK). Several special surveys were conducted in the period 1978 to 1985, and surveys of a more general character were only conducted in 1980 and 1983. From 1985 a new arrangement of three general surveys per year was put to use.  The reason for this alteration was the new media situation in the 1980ties that provided opportunities to choose between more and more channels on both radio and television. 

The most important purpose of the new survey arrangement was to provide data concerning: 
-	Changes in listener and viewer patterns over time
-	Variations in listener and viewer patterns in different seasons
-	Use and choice of different channels and program offers in radio and television
-	How people listen to radio and watch television
-	Access to radio and television sets and equipment that are used in connection to radio and television apparatuses. 

In addition to this, the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation wished to ask questions concerning people's opinions about different aspects of Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation program operations and concerning certain programs or program types.  The purpose was to conduct surveys that could give grounds for the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporations work in creating an offer of programs that listeners and viewers could benefit from, and that could contribute to illustrate the part radio and television play in people's lives. 

Three general surveys were conducted in 1985, in February, June and in September/October. This file consists of data from the June survey. NSD does not have access to data from the February survey. 

A gross sample of about 1500 interviewees was selected. These were interviewed in 7 groups about listener and viewer habits yesterday and the day before yesterday. The net sample amounted to 1136 interviewees. 

When mapping out time consumption, 24 hours was divided into fixed 10 minutes time intervals. Then activities for each time unit were registered, either it was spent listening to the radio, watching TV or doing other things. As a result, the two days could be put together, and one could let the interviewees be represented with two days in order to study time consumption. The number of units (days) was twice as much as the number of respondents and each person were therefore included two times in this dataset. This was done in order to include the interviewees answer for both days of programs in the same variable. This means that for instance the variable v156 “Hørte på radio 06.05 - KANALKODE” contains information about what the interviewee listened to on the radio at 06.05 o'clock on both days, both yesterday and the day before.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1985, 2nd quarter, new estimation factor
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others familiar with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS one respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, then there's a break for two quarters and then the respondent is interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10 to 11,000 respondents each quarter until the 1988. The  Labour Force Survey 1976 is the fifth full year with LFS. Originally it was intended that the LFS should be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics that were based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1975 SSB changed the selection plan for surveys, see respectively Art.37: On the use of random samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss) and SES 33: Principles and Methods of Statistics Norway samples (Ib Thomsen) Meanwhile, the estimation method for inflating national figures changed so that there are reasonable numbers for regions from the 1975 reorganization in 1975 led to interviews changing in how they were performed in the pools before and after. This meant, among other things a break from the Panel system used in the LFS.

By Labour Force Survey 1976, it was adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and we came back on the original six-quarter rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group and family workers temporarily absent from work were identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. Group seekers without work income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions of subordinates and employment in the original form was deleted

From Q1 1987 the estimation method (inflating the national figures) slightly changed, moreover, there was a minor adjustment to the definition of employment. To achieve comparability backwards in time was therefore developed new versions of LFS files back to 1980 From 1980 there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with the "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable to the original published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means of data for the period 1980-1987 which utilizes this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with the figures published after 1987

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: Depression and Personality, 1985
description: The project consists of an interview- and postal questionnaire survey as well as a diagnostic survey of 301 persons who have been treated at Psykritatisk poliklinikk at Vinderen, Oslo.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1984
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1984" marks the tenth anniversary of the first annual consumer survey conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the second of the panel period between 1983-1985.

title: Working Hours survey 1985, main file
description:
In 1984, the government appointed a committee to investigate the possibilities and effects of various future changes in working hours (the Working Hours Committee). The present interview survey was carried out by Statistics Norway on behalf of the same Working Time Committee, as a supplement to the Labor Force Survey 2nd quarter 1985. The purpose was to survey what kind of working time arrangements the working people have today, as well as gather information on attitudes to working time reforms. The survey contains information on working time arrangements, working conditions and attitudes to changes in working hours and retirement age. 

The data file consists of five different main elements: Variables with the prefix 'A' and 'B' originate respectively from the Labor Force Survey (LFS) 1st and 2nd quarter 1985. The Working Time Survey itself, which was an additional survey to the LFS 2nd quarter, can be found in variables with the prefix 'E' . Furthermore, variables beginning with 'C' are taken from the survey called Labor 1984 , which was an additional survey to the LFS 1st quarter 1985. Finally, we have variables beginning with 'D'. These are taken from the tax file of 1984. Other variables in the data file are derived by Statistics Norway. A separate "Spouse file" is also available, but it is not possible to link this to the interviewees in the Working Hours Survey.

title: Labour Force Survey 4. quarter 1983 - 1. quarter 1985, panel
description:
Labour Force Survey, 4. quarter 1983 - 1. quarter 1985, panel (6 quarters).

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (LFS). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research.  

In the Labour Force Surveys each respondent is interviewed for two subsequent quarters, followed up by a two quarter break. Then they are again followed up with two subsequent quarters.
The first complete edition of LFS is the Labour Force Survey of 1972. Such statistics was originally intended to be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics which were based on the National Insurance membership index. The statistics was based on the national insuranse index until the sickness benefit was included in the national insuranse system januar 1st 1971. Gradually LFS has evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the condition and trends in the labor market.

General information on the Labour Force Surveys: http://www.nsd.uib.no/data/ny_individ/norStudy/aku.html

Summary of AKU panels with detailed explanations for panel files:
Data collection for the LFS was started somewhat experimental in 1971, but the data are first published in 1972. It may be helpful to look at the following section of the detailed view:

Panel files are created by linking four interviews from an ordinary interview round of the LFS. In the table above this means one could connect all the pools (lines) labeled X. Participation 4th time is used as the criterion for the selection process.
In 1975 there was a break in the panel systematics, see 1975. The Labour Force Survey of 1976, adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and you came back to the original 6-quarterly rotation schedule which have been stable since.

If one looks at a particular column, for example 1st quarter of 1984, we will see that 1/4 of the respondents are included for the fourth time, for the third time, etc. The total sums the the material for one block. Quarterly weighting allows one such block (column) to be balanced with national figures. To give proper weighted figures for a annual volume consisting of four quarters, the quarterly weights has to be divides by 4.

The panels goes horizontally and each panel will have 4 weights, one for each quarter. Although they do not differ much, the weights will be prepared independently and each of them should be used for the data which the quarterly weight is made. Weighted figures apply to a pool, which means approx. 25% of the total quarterly sample, and one has to multiply by 4 to get the approximate national quarterly figures.

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation. 

This panel consider pool 52 in the table above.

Quarterly weighting allows one such block (column) to be balanced with national figures. To give proper weighted figures for a annual volume consisting of four quarters, the quarterly weights has to be divides by 4.

The panels goes horizontally and each panel will have 4 weights, one for each quarter. Although they do not differ much, the weights will be prepared independently and each of them should be used for the data which the quarterly weight is made. Weighted figures apply to a pool, which means approx. 25% of the total quarterly sample, and one has to multiply by 4 to get the approximate national quarterly figures.

By "Approximate national quarterly figures" it means that differences from the quarterly national weighted figures can appear, because of the selections and whitdrawal in the connection of the panels. Because of the whitdrawal inflated numbers will be lower than the national total figures. The main point of the weights is not to give national totals, but rather to ensure the best possible representation.

title: Norwegian Election Survey 1985
description: The Norwegian Election Research Programme was launched in the middle of the 1950's, under the direction of The Institute for Social Research. Henry Valen and Stein Rokkan were responsible for the programme. The programme co-operated with Norsk Gallup Institute A/S for sample survey collection in the election studies covering the Storting elections in 1957, 1965, 1969 and 1973. In these first studies interviews were carried out both before and after the elections. From 1977 the programme has co-operated with Statistics Norway (SSB) for the data collection in these studies. After 1977 interviewing has only been done after the elections. The 1985 election study was the seventh study to be carried out under this research programme. The election studies are intended to give statistical data for research covering elections and political processes in Norway. Since the start of the Norwegian Election Programme Professor Henry Valen has been the key administrator, but in 1985 Bernt Aardal took over as executive manager.

title: Diet Among Elderly Living at Home in Oslo, 1985
description: This project has dealt with the question of whether old age dementia leads to an insufficient diet or not. One aim has been to gain an impression of the general diet among the elderly in Oslo. Further on, the elderly who needed education about diets the most were identified and described. Information was gathered by interviews about medical and social objects, registering diets and blood analysis in order to achieve information about vitamin B status among the elderly. The sample consists of 255 elderly who live in their own home (as opposed to an institution).

title: Income- and Property Survey 1985, Household level data
description:
Statistics Norway carried out extensive income and property surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984 Statistics Norway switched to yearly surveys, this change mostly effected the sample size and sampling issues and not the type of information that is collected. The information is mostly decided based on publicly available taxation data. The income and property survey data is not a regular sampling survey, they are based on a selection that is drawn from the Tax Administration registries. Therefore, foreign citizens are included if they are part of the National registry, which all individuals with a work and residence permit normally are. Children born during the year are included regardless of date of birth, and the same is true of deceased individuals.

The sample is built on the idea that the data can be connected with the Level of living survey, Survey of consumer expenditure and similar surveys. The 1985 sample therefore consists of two independent and self-weighting samples, the sample for the yearly survey of consumer expenditure and a special senior sample over 84 years of age.

The purpose of the survey is to shine a light on the income of both the whole population and within different groups. One of the main goals is to create statistics about households, households being understood as those who live and eat together, and to create an overview of the distribution of individuals and household based on income size, socioeconomic groups, types of households, geography etc. Another main point is to collect income and property data as background information for the Level of Living and consumer expenditure surveys. Information on all types of income, assets, tax deductions, and disposable income for individuals and households. This material is mostly gathered from the tax registry from the tax directorate or from the municipal tax offices, and are therefore decided by the rules of tax calculation. Some information is gathered from other registers, for example marital status and family composition, while information about household composition is gathered through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than a household, a family can consist of single, unmarried fathers or mothers with children or married couples with or without children. A household consists of all individuals who lives and eats together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows that ca. 90% of all households consists of one family. (see NOS B 678) Tax free incomes like social benefits, gifts and profits are not included because of the rules of taxation. Rules for percentage taxation of property, valuation of benefits in kind, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc. also create a number of problems. 
In addition to tax information, information about education, occupation and industry of individuals are included. For households the composition, type and who the main income earner is, is included.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1984, 4th quarter
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others familiar with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS one respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, then there's a break for two quarters and then the respondent is interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10 to 11,000 respondents each quarter until the 1988. The  Labour Force Survey 1976 is the fifth full year with LFS. Originally it was intended that the LFS should be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics that were based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1975 SSB changed the selection plan for surveys, see respectively Art.37: On the use of random samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss) and SES 33: Principles and Methods of Statistics Norway samples (Ib Thomsen) Meanwhile, the estimation method for inflating national figures changed so that there are reasonable numbers for regions from the 1975 reorganization in 1975 led to interviews changing in how they were performed in the pools before and after. This meant, among other things a break from the Panel system used in the LFS.

By Labour Force Survey 1976, it was adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and we came back on the original six-quarter rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group and family workers temporarily absent from work were identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. Group seekers without work income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions of subordinates and employment in the original form was deleted

From Q1 1987 the estimation method (inflating the national figures) slightly changed, moreover, there was a minor adjustment to the definition of employment. To achieve comparability backwards in time was therefore developed new versions of LFS files back to 1980 From 1980 there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with the "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable to the original published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means of data for the period 1980-1987 which utilizes this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with the figures published after 1987

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1983
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1983" is the tenth in the series of annual surveys conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the first of the panel period between 1983-1985.

title: Labour Force Survey 1984, Year file
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others familiar with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS one respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, then there's a break for two quarters and then the respondent is interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10 to 11,000 respondents each quarter until the 1988. The  Labour Force Survey 1976 is the fifth full year with LFS. Originally it was intended that the LFS should be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics that were based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1975 SSB changed the selection plan for surveys, see respectively Art.37: On the use of random samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss) and SES 33: Principles and Methods of Statistics Norway samples (Ib Thomsen) Meanwhile, the estimation method for inflating national figures changed so that there are reasonable numbers for regions from the 1975 reorganization in 1975 led to interviews changing in how they were performed in the pools before and after. This meant, among other things a break from the Panel system used in the LFS.

By Labour Force Survey 1976, it was adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and we came back on the original six-quarter rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group and family workers temporarily absent from work were identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. Group seekers without work income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions of subordinates and employment in the original form was deleted

From Q1 1987 the estimation method (inflating the national figures) slightly changed, moreover, there was a minor adjustment to the definition of employment. To achieve comparability backwards in time was therefore developed new versions of LFS files back to 1980 From 1980 there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with the "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable to the original published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means of data for the period 1980-1987 which utilizes this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with the figures published after 1987

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1984, Household File
description:
Statistics Norway (SSB) conducted wide-ranging Income Studies in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. As of 1984, SSB moved to a system of annual surveys. This entailed changes, more in terms of sample size and sampling method than the type of information that was gathered. The information is mainly decided by what is available in public tax assessment records. The Income Studies should not be considered ordinary sample surveys, they are based on a sample drawn from registers from the Norwegian equivalent to the Internal Revenue Service. This means that foreign citizens would be part of the sample if they are registered in the Central Person Register, which they will normally be if they have valid work and residence permits. Children born during the year in question will be included in the sample regardless of birth date, and so will persons dying that the year.

The objective of the Income Studies have been to elucidate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A main point is to gather statistics on households that live and eat together, as well as getting an overview of the distribution of persons and households by size of income, socio-economic groups, household type, geography etc. Another objective is to collect income and fortune data as a backdrop for the Level of Living and Consumer surveys. Information is gathered om all types of income, fortune, taxes, income for individuals and households. Some information is added from other registers (amongst others, marital status and family composition, whilst information on household composition is gathered through interviews. In addition, one generally includes information on education, profession and occupation for individuals and household composition, type and primary income recipients for the households.  

There exist both a household file and a person file. These are documented separately. This is the household file.

Old means that data are comparable to the original published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means of data for the period 1980-1987 which utilizes this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with the figures published after 1987

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: Sociological Conditions Among Abortion-Seeking Women. An Epidemiological Study, 1983
description: The aim of the project is to display the effect of social conditions on the outcome of pregnancies. Data was gathered by interviewing 1090 abortion seeking and laboring women at the maternity hospital, regional hospital in Trondheim.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1982
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1982" is the ninth in the series of annual surveys conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
This study is the third, and last, of the panel period between 1980-1982.

title: Labour Force Sample Survey 1982, 4th quarter
description:
This dataset is based on the panel survey "Labour Force Sample Survey 1982, 4th quarter".
The Labour Force Survey will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. The Labour Force Survey has evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1996, the LFS questionnaire, file description, and standard coding of industry and occupation was significaly revised. Data collection eventually went over to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. The present file has incorporated these changes. New industrial classification in this file. (NOS C 182, based on EU standard NACE, Rev.1) New Classification of Occupations (STRYK) based on ISCO 88 is adopted from 1996, in these data the occupation are coded for new standard.

From 1996 the rotation schedule changed, so every family now is attending eight consecutive quarters. From 1996, also the sampling plan changed, making stratification at the county level.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1982, Individual Level Data
description:
Statistics Norway have conducted comprehensive Income and Property in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979 and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway changed to another system with annual surveys, however the changes made were mostly the sample sizes, and not the information collected. The information collected is mainly decided through the information available in the public taxation data. The income and property surveys are not considered ordinary sample surveys as the surveys are based on samples drawn from the Norwegian Tax Administrations registers. This entails that foreign nationals are included in the surveys if they are registered in the national register, which they would normally be if they have a working or residence permit. Children born in the course of the year will be included no matter their birth date, and the same applies for persons that deceased in the course of the year.

The Income and Property survey 1982 was distinguished from earlier income and property surveys - as the sample was influenced by the survey being connected to the Level of Living Survey 1983, the Survey of Consumer Expenditure 1982, The Labour Force Survey 1983 and part of the material for the elders Level of Living survey. The sample therefore consisted of 4 independent self-weighted samples, a total of 8.845 households or 24.453 persons. The purpose of the surveys has been to shed light on income situation for the whole population and for different groups. A main point is to create statistics concerning households that live and eat together, to create an outline of the distribution of persons and households after income size, socioeconomic groups, household type, geography, ect. Information concerning all forms of income, property, tax deduction, dispositional income for individuals and households was collected. This data was mainly collected from the Norwegian Tax Administrations registers. Some information was connected from other registers; this mainly concerns marital status and family compositions, while information concerning household compositions is collected through interviews. Family is a narrower term than household, a family can consist of singles, unmarried fathers or mothers with children, or married couples with or without children. A household can on the contrary include all persons that live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows however that around 90% of all households only consists of one family. (See NOS B 569). Tax-free incomes, as for instance social benefits, gifts and winnings will not be included because of the tax rules. Rules for imputed rental value/income of property, value determination of benefits, private outlets, and impairments etc. also created some problems. 

In addition to the tax information it is usually included information concerning among other things education, profession and industry for individuals and for the household: composition, type and the persons considered main provider. An important problem with the 1982-data was that the individuals-data file does not contain household numbers. That is why the two files on household and individual level cannot be connected.

title: The Time Budget Survey, 1980-81 (interview data)
description:
The purpose of "The Time Budget Survey 1980-81" is to gain insight into how the population spends their time on various activities.
"The Time Budget Survey" is our most important source of information concerning what forms of unpaid work is being performed in our society, who is performing this work and when it is performed. "The Time Budget Survey" containes information on hoe people actually live their lifes within the restrains caused by their living conditions, this is information no other survey gives, thus "The Time Budget Survey" is a valuable supplement to the data collected though "The Level of Living Surveys".      


"The Time Budget Survey" was conducted for the first time in Norway in 1972-1973 drawing heavyly upon the international survey "Comparative Time-Budget Projecet", executed as a comparative project in twelv countries(A. Szalai (red.): The Use of Time, 1965-66).  "The Time Budget Survey" 1980-911 is the second of its kind in Norway and is conducted in a manner making it comparable with the international survey

The survey addresses aspects concerning 
1. Work
2. Work related trevels
3. Domestic work
4. Personal needs
5. Education
6. Free time 

This dataset contains the data for the interviews. The diaries are documented in a separate file.

title: Labour Force Survey 1981, Year file
description:
Statistics Norway has carried out official quarterly Labor Force Surveys (AKU) from 1 quarter 1972. The purpose is to provide the labor authorities and others with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the AKU respondents are first interviewed in two consecutive quarters, then there is a delay of two quarters, and then the respondent are interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10- 11,000 respondents each quarter until 1988. Labour Force Survey 1975 is the fourth complete annual AKU. Originally it was intended that the AKU would be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics which were based on the insurance fund membership. The health insurance-based statistics, however fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971. AKU has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the labor market of the state.

In 1975 the SSB on the sampling plan for the surveys, see Art.37: About the use of samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss), and SES 33: Sampling Methods Applied by Statistics Norways sample surveys (Ib Thomsen). Meanwhile, the method of estimation for inflating the national figures changed, so that there are adequate numbers of regions from 1975. The reorganization in 1975 meant that the interview was carried out differently in groups than before and after. This meant, among other things, a break in the panel structure of the AKU.

The Labour Force Survey 1976 adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and came back to the original 6-quarters rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group, and family workers temporarily absent from work was identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. The group of job seekers without earned income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions about the under- and overemployment in the original form was deleted.

From 1. quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflating the national numbers) changed slightly, and also there was a minor adjustment of the definition of employed. To achieve comparability backwards in time, it was therefore developed new versions of the AKU files back to 1980. From 1980, there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable with the originally published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means that the data for the period 1980-1987 using this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with figures published after 1987.

This file is based on the new estimation method.

title: The Time Budget Survey 1980-81, diary data
description:
The purpose of "The Time Budget Survey 1980-81" is to gather a comprehensive overview over how the population spends its time on different activities. The Time Budget Surveys are our most important source of information about how much and what types of unpaid work are performed in society, who performs this work, and when it is performed. The Time Budget Surveys also contain data not found in other surveys, e.g., information about circadian rhythms, leisure activities, and time people spend with their children and the rest of the family.

The Time Budget Survey was first carried out in Norway in 1972-1973 and was originally inspired by the international survey "Comparative Time-Budget Projecet", where the same survey program was used in 12 different countries (A. Szalai (red.): The Use of Time, 1965-66). The 1980-81 survey is the second of its kind in Norway and is carried with a view to secure comparability with the results from the international survey.

The data is mainly collected by diaries kept by a selection of the population. In addition, participants are asked to answer questions in a face-to-face interview.

The survey consists of questions about the time use in then following areas:
1. Work
2. Work-related travels
3. Private work; hereunder housework, maintenance, childcare, purchasing and travels
4. Personal needs
5. Education
6. Leisure; hereunder sports and outdoor activities, entertainment, social interaction, media og reading

This dataset contains the data for the interviews. The diaries are documented in a separate file.

title: Norwegian Election Survey 1981
description: The Norwegian Election Research Programme was launched in the middle of the 1950's, under the direction of The Institute for Social Research. Henry Valen and Stein Rokkan were responsible for the programme. The programme co-operated with Norsk Gallup Institute A/S for sample survey data collection in the election studies covering the Storting elections in 1957, 1965, 1969 and 1973. In these first studies interviews were carried out both before and after the elections. Since 1977 the programme has co-operated with Statistics Norway (SSB) for the data collection for these studies. After 1977 interviewing has only been conducted after the elections. The election studies are intended to give statistical data for research purposes on elections and political processes in Norway. Since the start of the Norwegian Election Programme Professor Henry Valen has been the key administrator, but in 1985 Professor Bernt Aardal took over as executive manager.

title: Labour Force Survey. Ad hoc module 1. quarter 1981: Work 1980
description:
Labour Force Survey. Ad hoc module 1. quarter 1981: Work 1980
The ad hoc module on work in 1980 was conducted as a supplement to the Labour Force Sample Survey of 1981. It can be seen as a continuation of previous surveys on employment, conducted for 1976 and 1978. Similar studies were later conducted and prepared for analysis, for the years 1984, 1986 and 2007. The purpose was to provide a general overview of employment for the year 1980, provide information on absenteeism and job change and provide an overview of time spent in connection with work and various other activities such as housework, schooling and military service. The data file contains the variables from the AKU survey 1 quarter of 1981.


Labour Force Surveys
As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. The samples in AKU are from 1992 representative at county level. In the period 1972-1991 they were representative on county pair level. 

As from January 2006 some major changes were introduced to AKU  in order to enhance its comparability to similar surveys in other countries. The changes consist of minor definitional adjustments of unemployment, some adjustments and enlargement of the questionnaire and a change in age definition (age at reference point instead of at the end of the year). Simultaneously the lower age limit to be included in AKU was lowered from 16 to 15 years. This led to some breaks in the time series in the aforementioned areas.

--------------------------------------------
Originally, AKU respondents were interviewed in two consecutive quarters of a year, followed by a pause of two quarters, and then another two quarters of interviews. The sample was approximately 10-11.000 respondents in each quarter up until 1988. Originally, AKU was intended to be an analytical supplement to the monthly occupational statistics that was based on the social security membership index file. However, the social security-based statistics disappeared when the sickness benefit was included in the National Insurance as of 1st of January 1971, and AKU has after gradually developed into the most significant source of knowledge of the state of the labour market and its development. 

In 1975, Statistics Norway changed the sampling frame of survey research, see article 37: "Om bruk av stikkprøver ved kontoret for intervjuundersøkelser", SSB (About the Use of Random Samples at the Office for Survey Research, Statistics Norway) by Steinar Tamsfoss, and SØS 33: "Prinsipper og metoder for Statistisk sentralbyrås utvalgsundersøkelse (Principles and Methods for Statistics Norway's sample research) by Ib Thomsen. Simultaneously, the method for estimation of inflation to national numbers was changed, so that reasonable numbers for regions do exist from 1975 and onwards. The change in 1975 led to a different way of interviewing in groups. This caused amongst other things a break with the AKU panel systematics. 

In the AKU survey of 1976, a slightly changed questionnaire was introduced. Also, there was a return to the original 6-quarter rotation scheme. The new questionnaire implied a better identification of family workers and persons that are temporarily without paid work. Thus, 30-35 000 more people were defined as employed. The group of "job-seekers  without income" were also extended to include persons that were on an involuntary leave of absence. The questions concerning underemployment and "over employment" in the original questionnaire were abandoned. 

From the 1st quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflation to national numbers) was slightly changed. There was also a minor adjustment in the definition of employment. In order to ensure that the numbers were to be comparable to earlier surveys, new versions of the 1980-1986 AKU-files were drawn up. Consequently two versions of the 1980-1987 files - respectively with the old and new methods of estimation - exist. The "old" means that the data are comparable to the original numbers published in the period of 1972 - 1987, whilst the "new" implies that the data are comparable to numbers published after 1987. 
------------------------------------------------------------- 
Between the 1st and 2nd quarter of 1988, the AKU file description was changed. The variable "Labour-market status" was given a different coding. In addition, adjustments in the data collections were made - from interviewing a specific week every quarter to carry out continuous weekly interviews. SSB also started up an escalation scheme to increase the sample size. This affected the weights, and from the 2nd quarter of 1988, these were recalculated monthly. To balance out the quarterly or yearly files to total national numbers, the monthly weights therefore had to be divided in three or twelve to give the correct total number. 

In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. The data collection also changed to CATI - Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing. A new classification of industry was put into use (NOS C 182, based on the EU standard NACE, Rev.1). This standard was updated in 2002 and 2007. Also, the new Norwegian standard classification of occupations (STYRK) based on ISCO 88 was used from 1996 and onwards. The variable indicating socio-economic status was omitted, as a similar variable was not developed in the new occupational classification.

title: Labour Force Survey 1st quarter 1979 - 2nd quarter 1980 panel file, old estimation
description:
Statistics norway has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others knowledge about the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS respondents are interviewed two consecutive quarters, then after two more quarters they are interviewed again in two consecutive quarters. Labour Force Survey 1972 is the first complete LFS year long survey. Originally it was intended that such statistics would be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

title: The National Broadcasting System's User Survey, 1980, Form 10
description:
This data set is derived from the «The National Broadcasting System's User Survey, 1980, Form 10", conducted by Statistics Norway (SSB) on commission by the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation (NRK). The survey from 1980 was a part of Statistics Norway's annual listener and viewer survey conducted for the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation. Several special surveys were conducted in the period 1978 to 1985, while surveys of a more general character only were conducted in 1980 and 1983. From 1985, a new arrangement with three general surveys per year was put to use. The main purpose of the 1980-survey, like the previous general surveys, was to estimate the proportion of the adult population that followed certain programs on the radio and on television in the course of a particular study period, and to examine how the listeners and viewers were divided by background characteristics such as gender, age, education, profession and so on. 

Some questions concerning special topics were also asked in the 1980-survey. These special topics could be divided in the two following groups:
1.	What position does the radio have in people's everyday life? This was covered through questions concerning in what contexts people listened to the radio and how they assessed the program offer. 
2.	Comparing peoples radio listening habits to the use of other mass media such as newspapers, magazines, cassette players etc. 
This was especially important because one of the purposes of this survey was to study what effect the introduction of a “Program 2” would have on the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation itself and the radio habits of its audience.

12 types of forms were prepared for the survey. Every form contained questions concerning the programs for two particular program days (the two last days before the day of the interview). The 12 forms were divided into two main groups. In the first group of forms (group A), supplementary questions concerning the use of other media were included. In the other group (group B) there were some supplementary questions concerning radio usage. Furthermore, special versions of form A and B were developed for the two Mondays the interviews took place. This was done in order to cover the Saturdays in the same manner as the Sundays. A shortened version (C) was made for form B, and was used both Mondays. For form group A, a minor change was undertaken in the form that should be used 4/2 (D). The type of form that should be used for each interviewee was decided in connection to the sample draw. This was done to ensure that each program day should obtain information from an equal number of people. 

The survey period was the week Saturday 26th of January to Tuesday the 5th of February. This file contains data from the interviews conducted on Monday the 28th of January, and the questions concern the days Saturday the 26th of January and Sunday the 27th of January.

From 1985, a new arrangement with three general surveys per year was put to use. The main purpose of the 1980-survey, like the previous general surveys, was to estimate the proportion of the adult population that followed certain programs on the radio and on television in the course of a particular study period, and to examine how the listeners and viewers were divided by background characteristics such as gender, age, education, profession and so on. 

Some questions concerning special topics were also asked in the 1980-survey. These special topics could be divided in the two following groups:
1.	What position does the radio have in people's everyday life? This was covered through questions concerning in what contexts people listened to the radio and how they assessed the program offer. 
2.	Comparing peoples radio listening habits to the use of other mass media such as newspapers, magazines, cassette players etc. 
This was especially important because one of the purposes of this survey was to study what effect the introduction of a “Program 2” would have on the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation itself and the radio habits of its audience.

12 types of forms were prepared for the survey. Every form contained questions concerning the programs for two particular program days (the two last days before the day of the interview). The 12 forms were divided into two main groups. In the first group of forms (group A), supplementary questions concerning the use of other media were included. In the other group (group B) there were some supplementary questions concerning radio usage. Furthermore, special versions of form A and B were developed for the two Mondays the interviews took place. This was done in order to cover the Saturdays in the same manner as the Sundays. A shortened version (C) was made for form B, and was used both Mondays. For form group A, a minor change was undertaken in the form that should be used 4/2 (D). The type of form that should be used for each interviewee was decided in connection to the sample draw. This was done to ensure that each program day should obtain information from an equal number of people. 

The survey period was the week Saturday 26th of January to Tuesday the 5th of February. This file contains data from the interviews conducted on Wednesday the 6st of February, and the questions concern the days Monday the 4th of February and Tuesday the 5th of February.

title: Labour Force Survey 1980, Year file
description:
Statistics Norway has carried out official quarterly Labor Force Surveys (AKU) from 1 quarter 1972. The purpose is to provide the labor authorities and others with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the AKU respondents are first interviewed in two consecutive quarters, then there is a delay of two quarters, and then the respondent are interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10- 11,000 respondents each quarter until 1988. Labour Force Survey 1975 is the fourth complete annual AKU. Originally it was intended that the AKU would be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics which were based on the insurance fund membership. The health insurance-based statistics, however fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971. AKU has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the labor market of the state.

In 1975 the SSB on the sampling plan for the surveys, see Art.37: About the use of samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss), and SES 33: Sampling Methods Applied by Statistics Norways sample surveys (Ib Thomsen). Meanwhile, the method of estimation for inflating the national figures changed, so that there are adequate numbers of regions from 1975. The reorganization in 1975 meant that the interview was carried out differently in groups than before and after. This meant, among other things, a break in the panel structure of the AKU.

The Labour Force Survey 1976 adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and came back to the original 6-quarters rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group, and family workers temporarily absent from work was identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. The group of job seekers without earned income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions about the under- and overemployment in the original form was deleted.

From 1. quarter of 1987, the estimation method (inflating the national numbers) changed slightly, and also there was a minor adjustment of the definition of employed. To achieve comparability backwards in time, it was therefore developed new versions of the AKU files back to 1980. From 1980, there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable with the originally published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means that the data for the period 1980-1987 using this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with figures published after 1987.

This file is based on the new estimation method.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1978
description:
"The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1978" is the fourth in the series of annual surveys conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.
The questions in this survey are identical with The Consumer Expeniture Survey from 1976 and 1977.

title: Labour Force Survey 1977, 1.quarter
description:
Statistics Norway has carried out official quarterly Labor Force Surveys (AKU) from 1 quarter 1972. The purpose is to provide the labor authorities and others with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the AKU respondents are first interviewed in two consecutive quarters, then there is a delay of two quarters, and then the respondent are interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10- 11,000 respondents each quarter until 1988. Labour Force Survey 1975 is the fourth complete annual AKU. Originally it was intended that the AKU would be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics which were based on the insurance fund membership. The health insurance-based statistics, however fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971. AKU has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the labor market of the state.

In 1975 the SSB on the sampling plan for the surveys, see Art.37: About the use of samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss), and SES 33: Sampling Methods Applied by Statistics Norways sample surveys (Ib Thomsen). Meanwhile, the method of estimation for inflating the national figures changed, so that there are adequate numbers of regions from 1975. The reorganization in 1975 meant that the interview was carried out differently in groups than before and after. This meant, among other things, a break in the panel structure of the AKU.

The Labour Force Survey 1976 adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and came back to the original 6-quarters rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group, and family workers temporarily absent from work was identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. The group of job seekers without earned income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions about the under- and overemployment in the original form was deleted.

title: Fertility Survey, 1977
description: The fertility survey in 1977 was the first nationwide survey on fertility that Statistics Norway has carried out. The survey was part of a worldwide fertility survey led by the UN and other international organizations (World Fertility Survey). The purpose was to provide knowledge about the relationship between fertility and the factors that influence it, such as education and occupational activity, and to complement the descriptive statistics on fertility and demographic conditions. The survey was carried out with support from NAVF and FAD. In 1988, a similar survey was carried out, but this time greater emphasis was placed on the relationship with working life, and plans were made to collect more life course data. In 1977, only women were interviewed.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1976
description: "The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1976" is the third in the series of annual surveys conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. In this survey the log part is especially well documented. This leads to an extensive amount of information on houshold cosumption patterns. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.

In 1975 the SSB on the sampling plan for the surveys, see Art.37: About the use of samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss), and SES 33: Sampling Methods Applied by Statistics Norways sample surveys (Ib Thomsen). Meanwhile, the method of estimation for inflating the national figures changed, so that there are adequate numbers of regions from 1975. title: Labour Force Survey 1976, year file
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others familiar with the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS one respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, then there's a break for two quarters and then the respondent is interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The sample was approximately 10 to 11,000 respondents each quarter until the 1988. The  Labour Force Survey 1976 is the fifth full year with LFS. Originally it was intended that the LFS should be an analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics that were based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

In 1975 SSB changed the selection plan for surveys, see respectively Art.37: On the use of random samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss) and SES 33: Principles and Methods of Statistics Norway samples (Ib Thomsen) Meanwhile, the estimation method for inflating national figures changed so that there are reasonable numbers for regions from the 1975 reorganization in 1975 led to interviews changing in how they were performed in the pools before and after. This meant, among other things a break from the Panel system used in the LFS.

By Labour Force Survey 1976, it was adopted a slightly modified questionnaire and we came back on the original six-quarter rotation schedule. The new questionnaire meant that people in the group and family workers temporarily absent from work were identified in a better way. This resulted in approximately 30 to 35,000 more were counted as employed. Group seekers without work income was also expanded to include involuntarily laid off. The questions of subordinates and employment in the original form was deleted

From Q1 1987 the estimation method (inflating the national figures) slightly changed, moreover, there was a minor adjustment to the definition of employment. To achieve comparability backwards in time was therefore developed new versions of LFS files back to 1980 From 1980 there are therefore two versions of the files, respectively, with the "old" and "new" estimation method. Old means that data are comparable to the original published figures for the period 1972-1987, new means of data for the period 1980-1987 which utilizes this estimation factor (weight) is comparable with the figures published after 1987

This file is based on the NEW estimation method.

title: The Organization Survey - Elected trustees (who are not working full-time), 1975
description: This data file contains data from a survey that was sent out as part of the work on "Maktutredningen" which culminated in the public inquiry, NOU1982:3, in 1982. More specifically, this is data from the part of the organizational survey where elected trustees in central boards or corresponding bodies were interviewed. In 1976, a questionnaire was sent out to 114 organizations in the working and business world. In the organizations that were selected, there were 670 trustees who were sent a questionnaire. 475 of these responded to the survey. This gives a response rate of 71%. The survey was carried out to shed light on the relationship between organizational society and public policy. Data supports the claim that organizations in the labor and business world have power in Norway.

title: The Organization survey - Secretariat, Full-time employees and full-time trustees, 1975
description:
This data file contains data from a survey that was sent out as part of the work on Maktutredningen which culminated in the public inquiry, NOU1982:3, in 1982.
More specifically, it contains data from the organizational survey where full-time employees and trustees from the secretariat were interviewed.
In 1976, a questionnaire was sent out to 114 organizations in the working and business world in Norway. In the organizations that were selected, there were a total of 740 full-time employees in leading positions who were sent questionnaires. 536 of these answered. This gives a response rate of 72%.
The survey was carried out to shed light on the relationship between organizational society and the politics. Data supports the claim that the organizations in the working and business world has power in Norway.

title: Income- and Property Survey 1976
description:
Statistics Norway conducted comprehensive Income and Property Surveys in 1958, 1962, 1967, 1970, 1973, 1976, 1979, and 1982. From 1984, Statistics Norway switched to a system of annual surveys. This change mainly affected the sample size and sampling issues, with little impact on the type of information collected. The information is largely determined by what is available in public tax data. The Income and Wealth Surveys are not considered regular sample surveys; they are based on samples drawn from the tax authorities' registers. This means that foreign citizens are included if they are registered in the Central Population Register, which they usually are if they have work and residence permits. Children born during the year are included regardless of birth date, and the same applies to individuals who died during the year.

The purpose of the Income and Property Surveys has been to illuminate the income conditions for the entire population and for different groups. A key objective is to create statistics on household consumption, i.e., households that live and eat together, and to provide an overview of the distribution of individuals and households by income size, socioeconomic grouping, household type, geography, etc. Another key objective is to collect income and wealth data as a background for Living Conditions and Consumer Surveys. Information is collected on all forms of income, wealth, tax deductions, disposable income for individuals and households. Some information is linked from other registers, including marital status and family composition, while information on household composition is obtained through interviews. Family is a narrower concept than household; a family can consist of single individuals, unmarried parents with children, or married couples with or without children. A household, on the other hand, includes all persons who live and eat together and can therefore consist of several families. Experience shows, however, that about 90% of all households consist of only one family (see Notes 98/11 SSB 1998: Income and Wealth Survey 1995). Tax-free income, such as benefits of a predominantly social nature, gifts, and winnings are excluded due to tax rules. Rules for the percentage taxation of property, valuation of fringe benefits, private withdrawals, depreciation, etc., also create some problems.

In addition to the pure tax information, information on education, occupation, and industry for individuals, and for the household: composition, type, and who is the main income earner, is usually included. Data from the Education Register, the State Educational Loan Fund, the Housing Bank, the Ministry of Social Affairs (social assistance), and the National Insurance Administration are also linked.

title: The National Broadcasting System's User Survey, Form 7, 1975
description:
This data set is derived from the “The National Broadcasting System's User Survey, Form 7, 1975” that was conducted by Statistics Norway (SSB) commissioned by the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation (NRK). The survey from 1975 was one of SSB's annual listener and viewer surveys on behalf of NRK. Several special surveys were conducted in the period 1978 to 1985, while surveys of a more general character only were conducted in 1980 and 1983. A new arrangement with three general surveys per year was put to use from 1985. 

The main purpose of the 1975 survey, like the preceding general surveys, was to estimate the share of the country's adult population that followed certain program posts on the radio and television in the course of particular survey period, and how the listeners and viewers were divided by background characteristics such as gender, age, education and professions etc.  Information about how many that followed each program post entirely or partially and how many that started or ended their radio listening or television viewing in connection to the program post. Some questions that concern special topics were asked in the 1975 survey. This concerned among other things questions about changed broadcasting times for regular program posts, people's circadian rhythm, evaluation of the news coverage in different media and the possibilities for listening to the radio and watching television during the Easter holiday. 

7 types of forms were prepared for this survey. Each form contained questions concerning programs for one or two particular program days (the last or the two last days before the day of the interview). What type of form each person was to be interviewed with was decided in connection to the sample selection. This was done in a way that one for each program day could obtain information from a similar number of persons. 

The survey period was the week Saturday the 19th to Friday the 25th of April. This data file contains data for the interviews conducted Saturday the 26th of April, and the questions concern Friday the 25th of April.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1975
description: "The Consumer Expenditure Survey 1975" is the second in the series of annual surveys conducted by Statistics Norway. The data is organized in several sections and contains background information on the household (collected through an initial interview that maps the household composition), detailed consumption in terms of value and quantity (recorded by diary keeping, respectively for household and individuals), the households purchases of durable consumer goods (tangible capital goods, collected by a comprehensive exit interview), consumption of own products and income and taxes. The information presented is also used as the basis for calculating the CPI. Through the data collection the households are treated as independent respondents. For this purpose, a household was defined as persons living permanently in common housing with shared costs.

title: The Organization Study, 1975
description:
This is a survey carried out by Norsk Gallup A/S in 1975 on behalf of “Maktutredningen”. The topic was the population's participation in organisations.

This file contains data from the national survey that was carried out as part of “Maktutredningen” (NOU 1982:3), carried out on a representative sample of the Norwegian people. 2,203 people were interviewed by Norsk Gallup. The interviews were carried out in February-March 1975. The interviewees were to be over 15 years of age. The interview form only includes questions from “Maktutredningen”. The aim was to survey people's connection to organizations and associations. In addition, several questions about political activity and community involvement were taken up. The question about campaigns formed only a limited part of the overall survey (Olsen & Sætren, 1980).

The interview survey was to investigate the organized and their attitudes towards their organisation, other organisations, attitudes towards various issues and towards society in general. People's political activity was important, both through the traditional channels and campaigns. In addition, several questions were also asked to investigate the unorganized in Norwegian society. This group makes up around a third of the Norwegian population. This was one of the reasons why the sample group was made as large as 2203; the material needed be representative of two groups; the organized and the unorganized.

title: Labour Force Survey 1st - 4th quarter 1975, panel file
description:
This file contains the panel interviewed for the last time in the fourth quarter of 1975. Subjects were interviewed each quarter in 1975. The criteria for being included in the file that you attended in the last interview in sequence.

SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) as of Q1 1972. The purpose is to provide labor authorities and others information on the occupational structure of the population and development over time. The studies will provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS one respondent is interviewed for two consecutive quarters, so it is a two-quarter break and then the respondent is interviewed again for two consecutive quarters. The Labour Force Survey 1972 is the first complete LFS. Originally it was intended that such statistics would be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state and development of the labor market.

In 1975 SSB changed the sampling procedure for the surveys, see HIf Art.37: On the use of random samples at the office for interview surveys, Statistics Norway (Steinar Tamsfoss), and SES 33: Principles and methods for Statistics Norway samples (Ib Thomsen). Meanwhile, the estimation method for inflating the national figures changed so that there are reasonable numbers for regions from 1975. The change in 1975 led to the interviews changing in groups than before and after. This resulted among others a break in the panel structure of the LFS.

title: Consumer Expenditure Survey 1973, aggregated to household level
description:
Statistics Norway has used periodic consumption studies to develop the consumer price index. Detailed knowledge of the consumption of private households is also the foundation of quality of life studies, as well as studies concerning income, development, and e.g. the effects of indirect versus direct taxation schemes. In addition, the data is sufficiently detailed for dietary and nutritional analyses. Furthermore, the data is appropriate for follow-up surveys, models of economic planning, or for specification of private consumption in national accounts.

Large consumption surveys were conducted in 1958, 1967, and the survey of 1973 is the last of its kind. In 1974, SSB transitioned to a system of annual surveys with smaller samples. Between 1974 and 1976, data were published for three years at a time. The annual gross sample was approximately 1500 households, and the net samples were between 1100 - 1200 households. The objective to build a detailed overview of private consumption, which was to serve as the foundation of the weighting scheme of the consumer price index, remained. One also wanted to investigate the different consumption habits of different types of households. The households were stratified by income, main provider, employment status, age, neighbourhood, magnitude of total expenditures et cetera. The data is organized in several components and contain information concerning background (collected through an introductory interview), detailed consumption measured by value and magnitude (collected by respondents' consumption diary, individual and household), the household's purchases of durable consumer goods (perpetual capital goods, collected by concluding interview), consumption of self-produced goods, and income and taxation.

The consumption survey of 1973 was conducted using a subset of the sample of the income and wealth survey of the same year. The survey on living conditions was conducted using a further subset of the original sample. Due to the cumulative potential of these data, they have been used in a series of research projects. Consequently, NSD offers several versions of the dataset .

As of the 1st quarter of 1972, SSB has conducted official quarterly labour force surveys (AKU). These surveys aim to give the labour force authorities (and other people interested) knowledge of the occupational structure of the population and how it develops over time. The surveys are meant to give a foundation and statistical material for occupational prognoses and labour research. In 1996, AKU was significantly revised: The questionnaire, the file description and the standard for coding of industry and occupation. title: Labour Force Survey 1st quarter 1972 - 2nd quarter 1973, panel file
description:
SSB has conducted official quarterly labor force surveys (LFS) from Q1 1972. The survey aims to provide labor authorities and others knowledge about the occupational structure of population and development over time. The surveys provide the basis and statistical material for occupational forecasting and labor market research. In the LFS respondents are interviewed two consecutive quarters, then after two more quarters they are interviewed again in two consecutive quarters. Labour Force Survey 1972 is the first complete LFS year long survey. Originally it was intended that such statistics would be a more analytical supplement to the monthly employment statistics based on the insurance fund membership files. The insurance fund-based statistics, however, fell away when health insurance was included in the National Insurance Scheme from 1 January 1971, the LFS has gradually evolved to become the main source of knowledge about the state of the labor market.

This file covers data collected in 1st and 2nd quarter 1972 and 1st and 2nd quarter 1973. The criteria to be included in the file is that the respondent participated in the last interview sequence.

General information on the Labour Force Survey: http://www.nsd.uib.no/data/ny_individ/norStudy/aku.html
